title: #devex: 1739400021
preview:
Dev Patel: triage: we now have 3 auth-related customer reports across SDKs (SUP-4821 python, SUP-4884 ts edge streaming, SUP-4902 go newline). want to track counts per SDK for the QBR doc.
Ethan Park: python fix is merged as redwood-sdk-python pr-891 and released v0.9.4.
Dev Patel: TS fix in flight, will be redwood-sdk-typescript pr-512 (Authorization header dropped in edge streaming).
Elliot Price: Go fix is redwood-sdk-go pr-233; newline trim + clearer error.
Ava Chen: I just got another python one: customer passed api_key='Bearer <key>' and we double-prefixed -> 401. that's SUP-4933.
Logan Wright: we should add a shared doc: 'raw key only' + 'edge runtime header behavior'.
Ethan Park: +1. also, for reporting: question is customer-reported auth bugs, so SUP-4933 should count for python too even if it's config footgun.
Dev Patel: agreed. Ill update the internal sheet.

title: #eng-sre: 1739381107
preview:
Aisha Bello: trying to nail down which saturation signals the bot should *highlight* vs just attach as links. GPU util is noisy. queue depth seems actionable. KV cache pressure is… maybe. thoughts?

Logan Wright: +1 util alone is misleading. 95% util might be “healthy” if batching is doing its thing.
Logan Wright: what’s actionable is *util + symptoms* (p95 latency burn / 5xx / streaming disconnects) and a second saturation indicator.

Jacob Stein: agree. on the incidents I’ve worked, queue depth / scheduler backpressure was the “oh crap” signal.
Jacob Stein: GPU util is like… always 70-98% during busy hours.

Rafael Mendes: can we define “actionable” as: when it changes what we do in first 5 min? (reroute, shed load, scale, revert deploy)

Miles Thornton: for bot v2 we need simple heuristics. if we over-highlight, oncall ignores the card.

Allison Grant: we should also align w alert tuning. we already page on some of these; bot highlights should map to “this is the likely cause”.

Aisha Bello: ok, proposing categories:
- Highlight: queue depth/backpressure, admission control rejects, KV cache evictions (maybe)
- Mention but don’t highlight: GPU util, HBM/mem util
- Deep link only: per-GPU breakdowns, kernel-level stuff

Nadia Rahman: +1. also: be careful with metric definitions across hosted vs dedicated pools.
Nadia Rahman: for hosted, queue depth is at scheduler. for dedicated, some customers run a different batching config and queue depth behaves differently.

Logan Wright: metric candidates we can standardize:
- scheduler_queue_depth (p95 over 5m)
- scheduler_backpressure_ratio (time spent throttling / total)
- admission_control_reject_rate (per sec)
- gpu_sm_utilization (avg over 1m)
- kv_cache_evictions_rate OR kv_cache_pressure (whatever runtime exports)

Caleb Johnson: KV cache “pressure” is tricky. Some runtimes report it as a gauge but semantics differ.
Caleb Johnson: evictions rate is more actionable than “% used”. % used is always ~near cap by design.

Mina Haddad: from routing perspective, if bot highlights “regional saturation” we want it to suggest: route to alt region / enable fallback model variant / reduce max_tokens.
Mina Haddad: queue depth + backpressure is the cleanest input to that decision.

Ravi Menon: +1, and the degraded mode playbook basically keys off “are we queued + throttling” vs “are we failing for other reasons”.
Ravi Menon: GPU util doesn’t tell you if you’re queuing, it just tells you you’re busy.

Aisha Bello: thresholds? need something that triggers the yellow/red callouts in the card.

Allison Grant: current alert draft (not final):
- queue_depth_p95 > 2x baseline for 10m OR absolute > N (depends on pool)
- backpressure_ratio > 0.2 for 5m
- admission_control_reject_rate > 1% of requests for 5m
- kv_cache_evictions_rate elevated + latency burn

Jacob Stein: absolute queue depth thresholds are hard bc RPS varies. can we do time-in-queue instead?
Jacob Stein: e.g. “queue_wait_p95 > 200ms for 5m” would be more portable.

Logan Wright: we have queue wait time but it’s not consistently instrumented in dedicated yet.
Logan Wright: we can do both:
- if queue_wait_p95 exists: use that
- else fallback to queue_depth normalized by capacity (queue_depth / admitted_rps or / gpu_count)

Miles Thornton: bot logic needs to be deterministic + cheap. we can’t be computing baselines on the fly.
Miles Thornton: maybe thresholds are “static per pool tier” and we tune later.

Nadia Rahman: we can ship “v0 thresholds” with conservative highlights:
- RED: backpressure_ratio >= 0.35 OR admission rejects >= 3%
- YELLOW: backpressure_ratio 0.2-0.35 OR admission rejects 1-3%
- queue_depth used as supporting context, not primary highlight

Aisha Bello: what about GPU util? we still include it, but don’t highlight unless it’s low (to rule out saturation?)

Caleb Johnson: interesting angle: low GPU util + high queue depth = likely scheduler issue or upstream throttling / misconfig.
Caleb Johnson: but that’s an edge case. don’t bake too much magic.

Rafael Mendes: for first 5 min, simplest “if backpressure + rejects are high, it’s saturation” is good.
Rafael Mendes: queue depth alone causes too many false positives during deploys (warmup) and incident bot will scream constantly.

Allison Grant: +1. queue depth spikes are common during rolling restarts.
Allison Grant: but backpressure_ratio sustained is rare without real saturation.

Mina Haddad: also: if we highlight saturation, add a line that surfaces current routing policy + fallback enabled/disabled. that’s what oncall needs to decide action.

Jacob Stein: KV cache: what threshold would we use? eviction rate > X/s?

Logan Wright: runtime team suggested: “evictions > 0 for 5m” is already bad for some workloads, but it depends.
Logan Wright: maybe we highlight KV cache only when:
- evictions_rate > 0 AND p95 latency burn > 2x
- OR “kv_cache_hit_rate < 0.8” if available

Aisha Bello: ok, consensus check:
1) Primary highlight: scheduler backpressure_ratio + admission rejects
2) Secondary: queue_depth/queue_wait (supporting)
3) KV cache: highlight only if evictions + latency burn
4) GPU util: include but never highlight as “cause” alone

Miles Thornton: works for bot UX. we can implement “Highlights” block w 1-2 bullets max.
Miles Thornton: everything else in “Signals” block + dashboard links.

Rafael Mendes: pls add note about tuning thresholds per pool type (hosted shared vs dedicated) so we don’t break enterprise trust.

Allison Grant: I’ll paste in proposed wording for the card:
- “Saturation likely (scheduler throttling + rejects). Consider failover / scale / load shed.”
- “Saturation possible (queueing elevated). Check deploy + config changes.”

Aisha Bello: :thumbsup: I’ll update the standard doc + open a ticket for initial thresholds. Logan/Allison can you sanity check numbers once I draft?

Logan Wright: yup. also please keep query bounded: only pull p95 over 5m + last 30m sparkline link, no high-cardinality breakdown.

Caleb Johnson: +1 on bounded queries. per-tenant breakdown from bot will kill us during incidents.

Aisha Bello: cool. action items:
- Aisha: write threshold proposal + bot highlight rules
- Logan/Nadia: confirm metric names available in hosted + dedicated
- Allison: align with alert tuning so bot highlights match paging logic
- Miles: incorporate into bot card layout (highlight vs signals)

Miles Thornton: ack. will wire into v2 card gating (only show highlight if confidence high).

title: #eng-infra: 1739298841
preview:
Rafael Mendes: Heads up from yesterday’s incident review: incident-bot diagnostics caused a mini thundering herd on telemetry when multiple responders hit “refresh signals” in #incidents. We need hard bounds (rate limits, circuit breakers, backoff) before v2 goes wider.

Sean Gallagher: +1. This is exactly how we get cascading failures during Sev1/Sev0. What’s our current shape? Any global caps on diag queries?

Miles Thornton: Right now bot fans out to 3 backends (deploy-metadata, config-diff, telemetry-query). Telemetry is the big one: ~8–15 queries per card render depending on tenant/region selection. We have some basic per-process concurrency limit but no distributed/global limit.

Logan Wright: Telemetry side: the query gateway already has per-user limits, but bot uses a service identity so it bypasses “human” shaping. When the bot bursts it shows up as a single principal doing 200+ QPS.

Nadia Rahman: We can add a dedicated “diagnostics” quota bucket at the gateway keyed on service principal + route. But we also need the bot to degrade (serve cached summaries / links) instead of retrying forever.

Jacob Stein: From the SRE side we need two layers:
1) Client-side: concurrency cap + exponential backoff + request coalescing in the bot.
2) Server-side: token bucket rate limits + circuit breaker in telemetry-query-service.

Dylan Brooks: Agree. For v2 we’re already adding caching in the bot (ENG-5427) but infra guardrails should assume worst case (a bunch of people spamming buttons, and Slack retries).

Sean Gallagher: Can we decide numbers? Like max QPS for bot diagnostics per region? Also should be “per incident thread” so one noisy incident doesn’t starve everyone.

Logan Wright: Suggest:
- Global per-region cap for incident-bot principal at gateway: 20 QPS sustained, burst 60 for 10s.
- Additionally cap concurrency: max 25 in-flight per region.
We can tune based on p95 query latency.

Caleb Johnson: Also please cap cardinality / query complexity. Some of the proposed saturation queries use tag combos that explode. We should enforce a max time window and max series returned.

Miles Thornton: On bot side we can:
- Coalesce identical requests within 30s (same incident_id+service+region+window).
- Add a “cooldown” for the interactive refresh button (e.g., 60s per thread) so we don’t stampede.
- Set per-thread token bucket (2 refresh/min) with a friendly message.

Rafael Mendes: Love the per-thread bucket. We can treat the thread_ts as a key.

Nadia Rahman: Server-side: I can add a circuit breaker on telemetry gateway responses for the diagnostics route. Something like:
- Open breaker if error rate > 30% over 30s OR p95 latency > 8s over 2m.
- When open: return 429 with `Retry-After` and a “degraded_mode=true” header.

Dylan Brooks: If we can get a clear signal (429 + degraded header), the bot can immediately switch to cached context + deep links and stop retrying.

Sean Gallagher: This is important: no automatic retries on 429. Only retry on timeouts maybe, but with cap.

Sahana Iyer: Re: retries. Please don’t do “retry storms.” Rule of thumb:
- retry only idempotent GETs
- max 2 retries
- full jitter backoff (e.g., 250ms, 1s)
- and stop immediately on 429/403.

Connor O'Brien: Deploy metadata endpoint is cheap-ish, but during outages our internal service registry can be slow. Do we also want circuit breaking there?

Jacob Stein: Yes but smaller risk. Telemetry is the main blast radius. Still: a general “fanout budget” would help.

Miles Thornton: We can implement a per-card render budget:
- total render timeout 4s for initial message
- each backend gets 1.5s and we take partial results
- if any backend times out, show link and “try again later”
This keeps Slack UX usable.

Logan Wright: On telemetry query complexity: we can enforce:
- max lookback 30m for incident bot
- fixed aggregations (avg/max) rather than raw series
- max 50 series
If bot asks for more, return 400 with guidance.

Caleb Johnson: + also sanitize label sets. For example, do not allow freeform tenant_id OR request_id in metrics queries. Use our tenant-safe query primitive only.

Rafael Mendes: Ok, concrete action items:
1) Infra/telemetry (Logan/Nadia): gateway rate limit for incident-bot principal + diagnostics route caps (QPS + burst) + circuit breaker returning 429/Retry-After + degraded header.
2) Bot/tooling (Miles/Dylan): per-thread refresh cooldown + request coalescing + respect 429 (no retries) + retry policy w/ jitter.
3) Observability (Caleb): add dashboard for “diagnostics query load” and alert if bot is >X% of gateway traffic.

Sean Gallagher: Can we also add a circuit breaker “load shedding” mode on the bot itself? If it detects upstream unhealthy it should stop calling telemetry for a while.

Dylan Brooks: Yes. We can implement a simple client-side breaker:
- track last N telemetry calls
- if >50% are 429/timeouts over 60s, disable live queries for 5m and show cached+links.

Miles Thornton: I’ll wire that into the renderer. Copy for user:
“Diagnostics temporarily in degraded mode (telemetry under load). Showing cached signals from <time>. Use links for live dashboards.”

Grafana: (auto) Dashboard created: "Diagnostics / Query Gateway Load" https://grafana.redwood.example/d/diag-qg-load

Caleb Johnson: Nice. I’ll add panels for `qg_requests_total{principal="incident-bot"}` and p95 latency per route.

Nadia Rahman: One nuance: 429 vs 503. For circuit breaker, 429 is better (explicit backoff). 503 tends to get retried automatically by some clients.

Sahana Iyer: +1. Also ensure we set `Retry-After` in seconds.

Connor O'Brien: Deploy metadata endpoint: we can add local cache in bot too (deploy context doesn’t change minute-to-minute). TTL 2m?

Miles Thornton: Works. Deploy context TTL 2m, config diff TTL 5m (but invalidate if new config push event), telemetry TTL 30–60s.

Rafael Mendes: Any objections to the suggested limits? 20 QPS sustained / 60 burst per region for telemetry route?

Logan Wright: That’s fine as a starting point. If p95 goes up we’ll lower it; if it’s too strict we can raise after we see production usage.

Sean Gallagher: Make sure we have a “break glass” override for Sev0 where oncall can request a higher budget (but audited). Not required for first pass.

Dylan Brooks: We can do a manual flag for SRE-only to raise per-thread limit (still within global cap).

Jacob Stein: Please document the behavior in the runbook: responders should not spam refresh; rely on links if degraded.

Rafael Mendes: Cool. I’ll post a summary in #eng-sre and link this thread. Let’s target the gateway rate limit + bot 429 handling this week, before expanding the beta cohort.

incident-bot: (system) Reminder: When posting incident context, avoid sharing unredacted tenant config. Use approved “customer-safe” context cards only.

title: #architecture: 1739224450
preview:
Miles Thornton: quick write-up from the Incident Bot v2 arch review today. main question: can we ship deploy+config+sat signals without creating a second incident in the process :sweat_smile:

Miles Thornton: tl;dr proposal:
- bot -> diag-aggregator svc (new) -> (a) deploy-metadata svc, (b) config-diff svc, (c) telemetry-query svc
- aggregator does shaping + caching + rate limiting + degraded-mode behavior
- slack UX: 3 cards (Deploy / Config / Saturation) + “Open in Console” links

Rafael Mendes: +1 to putting all fan-out behind aggregator. oncall doesn’t care which upstream is sick, they just want “here’s what we know” and “here’s where to click”.

Logan Wright: biggest risk is telemetry load during real incidents. if #incidents has 40 ppl hammering “refresh context” we’ll DDOS ourselves.

Allison Grant: + paging loops. if bot causes extra latency in metrics pipeline, we’ll get more alerts, then more bot usage… spiral.

Ethan Park: question: is aggregator strictly read-only? any chance it triggers state (rollout pauses, routing changes) via interactive actions?

Miles Thornton: for v2, read-only. interactive actions are “expand diff”, “refresh”, “open dashboard”, “copy cli”. no “pause rollout” buttons.

Ravi Menon: read-only is good. we *should* still treat it as production-critical. during partial outages, it must fail predictably.

Devon Price: slack constraint: interactive action needs ACK in 3s. so aggregator has to respond fast or we do the pattern: immediate ack + “loading…” + async update.

Dylan Brooks: we’ve done the async update pattern in other bots. works, but we need idempotency keys so retries don’t post duplicate cards.

Connor O'Brien: deploy metadata path: we can provide last N deploys per service/region w/ rollout phase + canary status. join to PR/commit is possible but not always (hotfixes).

Benji Okafor: config diff: we can return “last 10 config mutations” and/or “diff between current and T-30m snapshot”. we need strong redaction hooks before anything hits Slack.

Marcus Lin: +1. we should assume Slack is not a secure sink. even in private channels. redaction must be server-side and testable.

Kevin Osei: and audit events. every call to config diff or privileged telemetry should emit an audit record (who/what incident/thread).

Naomi Feldman: from compliance side: we’ll want (a) scope-based access, (b) immutable audit trail, (c) clear break-glass rules. also define retention.

Miles Thornton: ok, diving into feedback requested:
1) data flow & boundaries
2) caching + coalescing
3) rate limits / circuit breakers
4) failure modes under partial outage

Ravi Menon: on (1): keep diag-aggregator dumb-ish. it should not encode “business logic” like routing decisions. it should *surface* current policy + pointers.

Mina Haddad: +1. if we embed policy eval in bot, we’ll drift from actual routing engine. better: bot shows pinned model, fallback chain, and “last routing policy change” link.

Jordan Lee: we can expose a “policy snapshot” endpoint that’s already computed by the control plane (safe subset). aggregator should fetch that.

Ethan Park: please make sure “safe subset” is explicit. policy configs can contain tenant annotations we don’t want sprayed in Slack.

Benji Okafor: for config diff, we’re planning allowlist-based rendering. everything else => “[redacted]”. secrets patterns are necessary but not sufficient.

Marcus Lin: exactly. regex-based “secret detection” will miss things and false-positive. allowlist primary, pattern-based secondary.

Allison Grant: on (2) caching: propose two layers?
- L1 in aggregator (in-memory) TTL 30-60s for interactive refresh spam
- L2 shared cache (redis) TTL 2-5m for same incident/thread to smooth bursts

Logan Wright: careful with caching telemetry. folks will treat it as real-time. if it’s 5m stale we need to label “as of” time and a refresh button.

Devon Price: UI-wise, we can show “Updated: 12:31:10Z” and “(cached)” in the card.

Rafael Mendes: + a note in degraded mode: “Live queries timing out; showing last known good context from 2m ago.”

Dylan Brooks: coalescing: yes. key should include {incident_id or thread_ts, service, region, tenant?}. if key is too broad we’ll leak tenant context.

Logan Wright: + on telemetry, avoid tenant in the query unless we need it. default to service/region aggregate for “saturation” card. tenant drill-down should be link out to Console where auth is stronger.

Miles Thornton: noted. current plan is:
- Saturation card = service/region/pool-level only
- Tenant-specific metrics only via deep link w/ RBAC

Aisha Bello: on (3) rate limits: we need budgets per thread and per user action.
- hard cap per thread: e.g. 6 refreshes / 5 min
- global cap in aggregator to protect upstreams
- backoff behavior: after cap hit, show message + links only

Allison Grant: also cap label cardinality. if telemetry query svc is asked for “by model, by tenant, by route, by status_code” we’re dead.

Logan Wright: we should define a strict set of queries the bot is allowed to run. like a fixed query catalog with parameters, not arbitrary promql.

Miles Thornton: yes, query catalog approach. aggregator calls telemetry-query svc with query_id + params (service/region/window). no raw query strings.

Ethan Park: +1. easier to permission and audit.

Kevin Osei: auditing: log (a) caller identity (bot service acct), (b) human user initiating action, (c) query_id, (d) params (redacted), (e) result size, (f) latency, (g) outcome (success/timeout/circuit-open).

Marcus Lin: and for config diff, log “fields returned” count maybe, plus whether any redactions occurred.

Benji Okafor: we can do “redaction_summary: N fields redacted” but keep it in audit logs not Slack.

Ravi Menon: on (4) partial outage behavior: define priorities.
- Must show *something* within ~10s (or slack will feel broken)
- If upstream slow: show cached + deep links
- If cached missing: show “manual commands” snippet (eg `rwctl deploys --service X --region Y`)

Connor O'Brien: for deploys, if deploy metadata svc is down, we can still pull from deploy event stream? maybe last-known state in redis.

Miles Thornton: we can cache deploy context longer (10-15m) since it doesn’t change second-to-second.

Allison Grant: but stamp it. “last deploy event seen at …”

Dylan Brooks: circuit breaker suggestion:
- per-upstream breaker in aggregator
- open when p95 latency > threshold or error rate > threshold
- when open: stop calling upstream for N seconds, serve cache + links

Logan Wright: yes. and telemetry-query svc should also have its own breaker to protect the TSDB.

Rafael Mendes: also, bot should never block incident channel during sev0. if it times out, it should say so plainly (not “unknown error”).

Devon Price: copy for error states:
- “Telemetry query timed out (10s). Showing cached context from 2m ago.”
- “Config diff unavailable: missing scope `config.diff.read`. Escalate via #help (INT ticket) or break-glass if Sev0/Sev1.”

Ethan Park: careful wording on break-glass. should route to the oncall/security process, not encourage random folks.

Naomi Feldman: agree. phrasing should be “Request elevated access via documented Sev0/Sev1 procedure (link).”

Kevin Osei: and the break-glass action itself must generate a high-signal audit event (and ideally page security-oncall if used outside sev0/1).

Miles Thornton: open question: should aggregator run in same cluster as bot or separate?

Logan Wright: separate. if bot gets wedged / restarts, we don’t want it taking cache + rate limiter with it.

Dylan Brooks: + separate deploy lets us roll diag changes without touching slack app bits. but keep it small.

Allison Grant: also consider: aggregator should have low dependency footprint. avoid calling 6 internal services. keep it to 3 + cache.

Priya Natarajan: platform POV: fine to add aggregator, but we need ownership clear. SRE owns? platform owns? shared?

Rafael Mendes: SRE can own runtime/SLIs, but data contracts live w/ respective teams. propose: SRE owns aggregator + bot, platform owns config/policy endpoints, telemetry owns telemetry-query svc, releases owns deploy metadata.

Miles Thornton: action items from review (please correct):
- [Logan] define query catalog + cost budgets; propose default windows + aggregations for saturation
- [Benji + Marcus + Naomi] finalize redaction + safe render allowlist + language for Slack
- [Allison + Aisha] rate limit + circuit breaker thresholds + “degraded mode” card standards
- [Connor] confirm deploy metadata schema + fallback plan when deploy svc unavailable
- [Kevin] audit event spec fields + retention
- [Devon + Dylan] slack async update + idempotency keys pattern for refresh/expand

Logan Wright: looks right. add: we should run a perf canary for telemetry-query latency as part of rollout.

Allison Grant: +1. and alert on “bot context generation latency” separately from core prod latency.

Ethan Park: please document permission scopes early. we’ve had surprises where bot can’t read prod config because role missing.

Miles Thornton: will add “scopes matrix” to the ADR. thanks all.

Marcus Lin: last ask: add tests that assert no forbidden fields can appear in Slack payload (golden tests). treat as security regression.

Benji Okafor: yes. we can add a suite w/ synthetic config that includes fake secrets + PII keys and assert output is redacted.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: ship it (safely).

title: #architecture: 1739128800
preview:
Allison Grant: Need a decision today on audit event shape: flattened fields vs nested objects. Main constraint: SIEM compatibility + keep schema stable for v1.

Kevin Osei: +1. From customer reqs: Sentinel + Splunk both *can* take JSON, but nested stuff often ends up as a string blob unless you add parsing rules. Also dot-notation keys can get weird depending on pipeline.

Logan Wright: For coverage dashboard + queries, flat is easier. Nested means our metrics extraction has to handle multiple shapes (and people will inevitably log arbitrary JSON).

Nadia Rahman: But we still need a way to carry producer-specific details (e.g., policy diff summary, installer operator metadata) without bloating the canonical field list.

Marcus Lin: Security POV: we care about consistent actor/target + correlation first. Anything that breaks queries during an incident is a no.

Ethan Park: Control plane already has objects internally (`actor`, `target`, etc.). Flattening will be annoying but doable. My concern is “flattened forever” makes future evolution painful.

Allison Grant: Dropping some examples for discussion.

Allison Grant: Option A (nested-ish):
```json
{
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "event_id": "01J...",
  "event_type": "rbac.role.update",
  "occurred_at": "2026-02-10T19:03:12.123Z",
  "tenant_id": "tnt_...",
  "actor": { "type": "user", "id": "usr_...", "email": "a@b.com" },
  "target": { "type": "role", "id": "role_..." },
  "request": { "request_id": "req_...", "trace_id": "trc_...", "source_ip": "1.2.3.4" },
  "extensions": { "policy_engine": { "diff": "..." } }
}
```

Allison Grant: Option B (flat):
```json
{
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "event_id": "01J...",
  "event_type": "rbac.role.update",
  "occurred_at": "2026-02-10T19:03:12.123Z",
  "tenant_id": "tnt_...",
  "actor_type": "user",
  "actor_id": "usr_...",
  "actor_email_hash": "sha256:...",
  "target_type": "role",
  "target_id": "role_...",
  "request_id": "req_...",
  "trace_id": "trc_...",
  "source_ip": "1.2.3.4",
  "ext_policy_engine_diff": "..."
}
```

Kevin Osei: Strongly prefer Option B for SIEM export defaults. Customers can still parse JSON, but they shouldn’t have to.

Jordan Lee: Re: ext fields — can we use dotted names like `ext.policy_engine.diff`? Some SIEMs treat dot as nesting. Might be okay, but risky.

Kevin Osei: Yeah, Splunk: dotted fields become painful unless you normalize. Sentinel: depends on KQL ingestion rules. My vote: underscore prefix for namespacing: `ext_policy_engine_*`.

Hanae Suzuki: Also data classification/redaction is cleaner with flat + explicit fields. If we allow arbitrary nested “extensions”, people will accidentally drop raw payloads in there.

Ethan Park: Counterpoint: forcing everything into `ext_*` strings feels like a junk drawer. Can we at least constrain ext keys via registry (namespaces + allowed fields)?

Logan Wright: We can do that. Registry can include canonical fields + allowed extension keys per namespace. Telemetry can track unknown keys to catch drift.

Allison Grant: Another angle: query ergonomics. In incident response we want: `event_type=... actor_id=... target_id=... source_ip=...` without JSON-path.

Marcus Lin: +1. During an investigation, people will be in Splunk/Sentinel at 3am. Flat fields win.

Nadia Rahman: If we go flat, can we keep a stable top-level set and allow extensions but still avoid huge cardinality? Some fields like `diff` can explode.

Allison Grant: We’ll enforce cardinality constraints in the standard (cap lengths, forbid free-form blobs unless explicitly approved). For diffs we can store a summarized diff + optional hash pointer.

Jordan Lee: So for policy changes: `ext_policy_engine_diff_summary` + `ext_policy_engine_diff_sha256`? Then full diff goes to internal debug logs only, not audit.

Hanae Suzuki: Works for PII too. If someone wants `actor_email`, we say no; only `actor_email_hash` if needed for correlation.

Ethan Park: Implementation detail: control plane services already build an `AuditEvent` struct. If we pick flat, we should provide a lib that takes nested internal structs and outputs the flattened schema.

Allison Grant: That’s already in flight (schema library + validator). We can add helper methods: `SetActor(...)`, `SetTarget(...)`, `SetRequestContext(...)`, and `AddExtension(namespace, key, value)` which emits `ext_<namespace>_<key>`.

Kevin Osei: +1 please also add exporter normalization: if some legacy producer still emits `actor.id`, exporter maps to `actor_id` etc.

Logan Wright: For coverage dashboard: we’ll key off `event_type` + `schema_version` + `service_name` (also needs to be top-level). If `service_name` is nested we’re back to parsing.

Allison Grant: Good call: `service_name`, `service_version`, `deployment_mode` (hosted/dedicated/private) should be top-level.

Marcus Lin: Any objections to: “Canonical top-level stable fields + flat namespaced extensions, no nested objects in v1”? (Exceptions only if a field is *always* a scalar and widely supported.)

Ethan Park: I’m good with that as long as we: (1) reserve prefixes, (2) provide registry + lints, (3) document how to evolve without breaking.

Nadia Rahman: +1. Also let’s explicitly state “extensions are optional; consumers must not rely on them for core audit semantics.”

Kevin Osei: Works. From customer side we can say: stable top-level schema for SIEM; optional extensions for richer context.

Allison Grant: Decision: v1 will use **flattened top-level canonical fields** and allow **namespaced extensions as additional top-level fields** using `ext_<namespace>_<field>` (underscores, not dots). No nested JSON objects for actor/request/target; represent those as scalar fields (`actor_type`, `actor_id`, etc.). We’ll capture this in the ADR + logging standard and update the schema draft accordingly.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: I’ll update the coverage extractor to whitelist `ext_*` but ignore them for the “required fields present” check.

Hanae Suzuki: :+1: I’ll add redaction guidance: extensions cannot include secrets/PII; require review for any new `ext_*` key.

Kevin Osei: I’ll note in exporter docs: if customer wants nested output, they can transform downstream, but Redwood export is flat by default.

Allison Grant: Cool. I’ll post a follow-up with the reserved namespaces list (console, control_plane, policy_engine, private_installer, gateway, exporter) and the lint rule proposal.

Allison Grant: That’s already in flight (schema library + validator). We can add helper methods: `SetActor(...)`, `SetTarget(...)`, `SetRequestContext(...)`, and `AddExtension(namespace, key, value)` which emits `ext_<namespace>_<key>`.

Kevin Osei: +1 please also add exporter normalization: if some legacy producer still emits `actor.id`, exporter maps to `actor_id` etc.

Logan Wright: For coverage dashboard: we’ll key off `event_type` + `schema_version` + `service_name` (also needs to be top-level). If `service_name` is nested we’re back to parsing.

Allison Grant: Good call: `service_name`, `service_version`, `deployment_mode` (hosted/dedicated/private) should be top-level.

Marcus Lin: Any objections to: “Canonical top-level stable fields + flat namespaced extensions, no nested objects in v1”? (Exceptions only if a field is *always* a scalar and widely supported.)

Ethan Park: I’m good with that as long as we: (1) reserve prefixes, (2) provide registry + lints, (3) document how to evolve without breaking.

Nadia Rahman: +1. Also let’s explicitly state “extensions are optional; consumers must not rely on them for core audit semantics.”

Kevin Osei: Works. From customer side we can say: stable top-level schema for SIEM; optional extensions for richer context.

Allison Grant: Decision: v1 will use **flattened top-level canonical fields** and allow **namespaced extensions as additional top-level fields** using `ext_<namespace>_<field>` (underscores, not dots). No nested JSON objects for actor/request/target; represent those as scalar fields (`actor_type`, `actor_id`, etc.). We’ll capture this in the ADR + logging standard and update the schema draft accordingly.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: I’ll update the coverage extractor to whitelist `ext_*` but ignore them for the “required fields present” check.

Hanae Suzuki: :+1: I’ll add redaction guidance: extensions cannot include secrets/PII; require review for any new `ext_*` key.

Kevin Osei: I’ll note in exporter docs: if customer wants nested output, they can transform downstream, but Redwood export is flat by default.

Allison Grant: Cool. I’ll post a follow-up with the reserved namespaces list (console, control_plane, policy_engine, private_installer, gateway, exporter) and the lint rule proposal.

title: #docs: 1739059440
preview:
Marissa Cole: hey docs folks + eng — can we get eyes on the Route Experiments customer guide before we merge? PR here: https://github.com/redwood/redwood-docs/pull/1447

Marissa Cole: specifically want feedback on troubleshooting section + whether our examples are clear enough for beta customers (we got a bunch of “what do these headers mean” last week)

Redwood Docs Bot: PR #1447 build preview ready: https://docs-preview.redwood.ai/pr-1447/route-experiments

Dylan Brooks: :eyes: taking a pass now. first impression: overall solid, but troubleshooting needs a “symptoms -> likely cause -> what to check” table. right now it’s more narrative.

Felix Schneider: +1. also think we should add one screenshot callout in Results page about “data freshness” / aggregation delay. Support ticket SUP-24910 was basically “results not updating” and it was just rollup job lag.

Chloe Martin: skimmed quickly — API bits look ok but we should be precise about stop semantics.

Chloe Martin: docs currently say “Stop immediately removes traffic.” in reality we have draining for some routes (depending on streaming) + control plane propagation lag. can we reword to “Stops new assignments; in-flight requests may complete” + mention typical propagation window?

Rafael Mendes: +1 and please include an explicit “guardrails are soft-stop by default” statement. we keep getting assumptions that it will hard-stop and revert automatically.

Marissa Cole: yep, agreed. we can add “soft-stop by default; hard-stop optional (if enabled by org admin)” with a link to guardrails page.

Jordan Lee: deterministic assignment section: can we be explicit about stickiness key precedence? people keep passing random IDs and then wonder why split looks skewed.

Jordan Lee: current precedence (per ADR-047):
1) explicit `x-redwood-sticky-key` header (or SDK equivalent)
2) customer-provided user id header if configured (some enterprise gateways)
3) fallback generated key (NOT recommended; low entropy risk)

Jordan Lee: also mention: if you don’t provide a stable key, you *should not* expect stable assignment across requests.

Logan Wright: + telemetry side: we should add a troubleshooting bullet for “attribution drop” esp on streaming + retries. we fixed one path (PR 28622), but customers still can see missing `experiment_id/variant_id` if they’re sampling spans aggressively.

Logan Wright: suggestion: include a short snippet for checking response headers and note that metrics attribution is based on server-side context; headers are for debugging only.

Allison Grant: please also add the cardinality note. we intentionally do NOT tag every metric with every dimension. customers ask “why can’t i break down by user_id” — we need to set expectations.

Allison Grant: maybe a callout box:
- Always available: `{route, experiment_id, variant_id, region}`
- Sometimes available / sampled: `{model_id}`
- Not available: arbitrary user attributes

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC section: the guide says “anyone with Console access can stop an experiment.” that’s not right.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: minimum roles:
- Viewer: can view results + config
- Operator: can start/stop/ramp traffic
- Admin: can create/update/delete + enable hard-stop policy

Kaitlyn Nguyen: (names might differ in UI but we should at least say “depends on org RBAC” + point to permissions page)

Connor O'Brien: release note-ish: can we add a “Beta limitations” section? for GA checklist we’ve been repeating:
- no statistical significance engine
- no custom metrics ingestion
- results can lag by X minutes

Connor O'Brien: it helps set expectations so CS doesn’t have to explain on every call.

Dylan Brooks: specific copy edits:
- “variant headers” section should show exact header names (case sensitive) + an example response.

Dylan Brooks: right now it says “we include variant headers.” i’d add:
```
# response headers (debugging)
x-redwood-experiment-id: exp_2f8c...
x-redwood-experiment-variant-id: var_b
x-redwood-route-id: route_chat_completions
```

Dylan Brooks: also show how to echo request id so support can find it.

Marissa Cole: ok. can someone paste a minimal curl example we can drop in?

Jordan Lee: here’s a simple one (no auth shown):
```
curl -sS https://api.redwood.ai/v1/chat/completions \
  -H 'content-type: application/json' \
  -H 'x-redwood-sticky-key: user_12345' \
  -d '{
    "model": "redwood/llama-3.1-70b-instruct",
    "messages": [{"role":"user","content":"Write a 2 sentence summary of Redwood Inference."}],
    "stream": false
  }' -D -
```

Jordan Lee: `-D -` is key since it prints headers.

Logan Wright: troubleshooting bullets i’d add verbatim:
- “I see 90/10 configured but my dashboard looks 100/0”: usually missing or low-entropy sticky key -> check request headers; also look for “variant 0 traffic” alert.
- “Console shows no data for variant”: attribution drop or experiment not started in that region.
- “Metrics don’t match my client timing”: Console uses server-side latency (router->completion), client includes network.

Allison Grant: can we include the metric definitions? just a quick one-liner each:
- latency p95/p99 = server-side request duration
- cost/token = billed tokens * unit price (may differ by model)
- error rate = non-2xx + router errors

Rafael Mendes: and for “results stale”:
- check config propagation lag metric
- check rollup job delay
- what to do: wait X min; if > threshold, contact support with experiment_id + region

Chloe Martin: + “stop semantics” snippet:
- Stop prevents *new* assignments.
- Existing sticky users may still route to prior variant until propagation completes.
- Typical propagation < 60s; worst case bounded by staleness SLO.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: re: internal debug endpoint — docs should NOT mention it. customers asked. it’s internal-only and RBAC gated.

Marissa Cole: yep, will keep internal-only. we’ll just document headers + Console.

Felix Schneider: one more: in Results view we show “Guardrail breached” banner w/ link to runbook internally. for customer docs we should explain “guardrail status is evaluated on rolling window; may clear after traffic reduces.”

Marissa Cole: makes sense.

Connor O'Brien: can we reference the sample app PR too? helps customers copy/paste.

Dylan Brooks: yep. https://github.com/redwood/redwood-examples/pull/608 — it demonstrates sticky key + prints assigned variant.

Dylan Brooks: also TypeScript SDK helper is in https://github.com/redwood/redwood-sdk-typescript/pull/533 and python in https://github.com/redwood/redwood-sdk-python/pull/721 — maybe link under “SDK notes”.

Marissa Cole: action items i’m taking from this thread:
- add “Beta limitations” section (no stat sig, no custom metrics)
- add troubleshooting table (symptom/cause/check)
- add explicit header names + curl example (with `-D -`)
- clarify stop semantics + propagation lag expectations
- clarify guardrails default = soft-stop, hard-stop optional
- fix RBAC wording (viewer/operator/admin)
- add metric definitions + cardinality note

Marissa Cole: i’ll push edits in the PR today and re-request review. thanks all.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: ping me when updated, i’ll re-check the metric defs + cardinality callout.

Logan Wright: same, i’ll review the attribution/troubleshooting bits.

Rafael Mendes: and i’ll look at the guardrails wording so it matches what oncall expects.

Redwood Docs Bot: Marissa Cole pushed commits to PR #1447: "Clarify stop semantics + add troubleshooting table"

title: #eng: 1738960013
preview:
Connor O'Brien: pulling folks into one thread re: feature flag + cohort mechanics for onboarding checklist MVP. We need to lock this this week for rollout plan + Support readiness. PR is up (pr-28467) but there are open questions.

Dylan Brooks: thx. current implementation in Console uses `onboarding_checklist_enabled` from config service. if false, we hide entrypoint + don’t call progress API. if true, UI loads and renders whatever state.

Chloe Martin: backend side (progress svc) is always on in prod (since gateway can emit events regardless). but we can gate the *Console access* + step details. I’m +1 to not gating event emission; otherwise we create data gaps.

Logan Wright: +1. telemetry wants events regardless so we can baseline funnel / TTFS pre/post. gating event emission by flag breaks comparability. we can add `flag_variant`/`exposure` as context in the events if we need.

Ben Carter: for cohorting, we should decide: tenant-level or user-level. Product asked for “accounts see checklist” not “some users in same org see different things”. I strongly recommend tenant-level.

Marissa Cole: tenant-level is the intent. we don’t want a support scenario where admin sees checklist and other teammate doesn’t.

Nikhil Sharma: agree. also for TTFS/TTFP reporting we care per tenant, so exposure should be stable.

Connor O'Brien: ok tenant-level. question: where do we compute cohort? console side vs server side.

Ethan Park: compute server-side in control plane/config. if Console computes cohort locally you can get drift (different SDK versions, different hashing). Better: config service returns `onboardingChecklist: { enabled: true, variant: "A", cohort: "dogfood" }`.

Dylan Brooks: today we’re calling `GET /v1/console/config` and it returns feature gates already. we can extend.

Talia Benson: also helps with “kill switch” - one place to flip.

Connor O'Brien: cool. what’s the cohort rule? what fields do we use for deterministic bucketing.

Ben Carter: do NOT use email domain. use `tenant_id` stable hash. segmentation rules layered on top:
1) allowlist internal dogfood tenants
2) allowlist a few design partners
3) then % rollout by tenant_id hash bucket
We can also exclude “enterprise” plan if we want, but we need clarity.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE perspective, % rollout must be a real kill switch (0%) and must be fast to propagate (<5 min). also: if there’s an outage in progress svc, the flag should fail closed (hide UI) not degrade console performance.

Chloe Martin: progress svc is behind the same edge as Console API. if it errors, UI can show “Checklist temporarily unavailable” and still render rest of Console. We can also have Console only call it if enabled.

Allison Grant: we should treat it as non-critical path. timeouts should be low (e.g., 300-500ms) + no retries from browser. avoid cascading issues.

Dylan Brooks: yup, I set 400ms timeout and on failure we drop the component (no blocking). we still log a metric + Sentry event.

Nadia Rahman: re: adding `flag_variant` to events: prefer server-side enrichment. gateway emits events; telemetry pipeline can join against exposure dataset keyed by tenant_id + timestamp. otherwise you leak flag evaluation details into multiple emitters.

Logan Wright: +1. we can write an “exposure snapshot” table in analytics daily + also a real-time KV for joins. but simplest: config svc also emits `onboarding.checklist.exposed` event when enabled flips from false->true for a tenant.

Connor O'Brien: do we need an explicit exposure event for counting? or can we just use config snapshot.

Logan Wright: for accurate funnel denominators, explicit exposure event is nice. but we can ship MVP with snapshot and add event later.

Marissa Cole: I’m ok with snapshot for MVP as long as we can answer “who saw the checklist” reliably. Support will ask.

Amara Diallo: question: if we don’t gate gateway event emission, do we risk “step complete” happening for tenants who never saw checklist? ex: api key created, first 2xx request, etc.

Chloe Martin: that’s fine. progress state is basically computed from behavior; showing UI is separate. if tenant later gets exposed, their checklist will appear partially complete (which might be good!).

Ben Carter: agree. and it reduces “cold start” confusion.

Nikhil Sharma: BUT: need to make sure step ordering doesn’t look weird if they already did prod stuff. e.g., “Set budget alerts” might be incomplete while “Send production traffic” looks complete.

Dylan Brooks: UX spec has “Recommended next” behavior. If some advanced steps are complete, we still show sequence but highlight the first incomplete. Also can collapse completed steps.

Talia Benson: ok. rollout mechanics: what cohorts for phase 1 dogfood?

Connor O'Brien: proposal:
- Week 1 internal (Redwood tenants) + 3 handpicked partner tenants
- Then 5% new self-serve signups (created_at >= rollout date)
- Then 25% all eligible
Need to decide if we only target *new* tenants or include existing.

Ben Carter: for activation lift we care mainly new. I’d do new-only initially to avoid surprising existing customers in Console. But telemetry wants some existing for baseline comparisons.

Logan Wright: baseline comparisons don’t require exposure; we can compare pre vs post on new signups only. I’m ok with new-only for the UI.

Marissa Cole: new-only simplifies Support training too (“If they signed up after X they probably have it”).

Sean Gallagher: new-only reduces blast radius. do it.

Chloe Martin: implementing “new-only” requires we have tenant.created_at in config service. do we already have that accessible?

Ethan Park: yes in control plane. config svc can evaluate rule `created_at >= 2025-02-xx`.

Dylan Brooks: one nuance: if we gate on created_at, tenants created just before date never get it unless we run a migration/override. maybe we want an allowlist for “requested access”.

Connor O'Brien: we can include an override list. Support asked for this anyway (ENG-5852 admin override is separate though). For rollout gating, we can maintain a “rollout allowlist” in config.

Ben Carter: yes. allowlist should be in control plane, editable by oncall/Release coord.

Allison Grant: please put guardrails: any manual allowlist change should be audited. doesn’t need full SOC2 today but at least who/when.

Ethan Park: we can log changes in config admin logs.

LaunchDarkly Bot: LaunchDarkly flag update: `console.onboarding_checklist` created in project `redwood-console`. Default: off. Rules: allowlist (internal tenants), then 0% rollout.

Dylan Brooks: wait we’re using LaunchDarkly directly? I thought we were keeping feature flags in control plane config.

Connor O'Brien: sorry, to clarify: pr-28467 uses LD SDK server-side in config service. Console reads config service, not LD directly.

Chloe Martin: ok that’s fine.

Nadia Rahman: if LD is used, please ensure config service caches evaluation results (per tenant) w/ TTL. don’t want every console page load hammering LD.

Ethan Park: caching is in place. We cache per tenant_id for 5 minutes; bust cache on config change webhook.

Sean Gallagher: 5 minutes ok. make sure kill switch propagation meets SRE ask. webhook path must be reliable.

Ethan Park: webhook already used for other flags. if webhook fails, worst case TTL 5 min.

Connor O'Brien: decision points to record:
1) tenant-level bucketing
2) config service evaluates flag (LD backed)
3) UI gated, event emission not gated
4) new tenants only initially + allowlist override
5) fail closed if progress svc down (hide component)
Anything else?

Marissa Cole: also: exclude enterprise contracts at first? I’d exclude anything with `plan_tier=enterprise` until Support + CS trained.

Ben Carter: I’d exclude enterprise by default, but allowlist for design partners.

Connor O'Brien: ok. Ethan can add rule in config service.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll update Console to display a small “Beta” pill only for dogfood cohort? or for all. UX spec says avoid “beta” unless we think it’s unstable.

Nikhil Sharma: avoid beta label; it makes customers nervous. use normal UI.

Talia Benson: timeline: can we land the rule changes today so we can start dogfood tomorrow?

Ethan Park: yes if decisions final. I’ll update config rules + add fields returned to Console: `enabled`, `cohort`, `rollout_reason` (for debugging; not shown in UI).

Allison Grant: careful with `rollout_reason` surfacing to client. don’t leak internal segmentation logic. keep it server logs only.

Dylan Brooks: +1. we can keep it behind an internal header or only in logs.

Connor O'Brien: ok. action items:
- Ethan: implement new-only + enterprise exclude + allowlist; keep reason server-side
- Dylan: ensure progress API calls are gated + timeout/fail closed + no retries
- Logan/Nadia: confirm telemetry approach for exposure denominator (snapshot ok MVP)
- Connor: update PM-768 + rollout doc with these decisions

Logan Wright: I’ll post in #eng-platform that telemetry is ok with snapshot for MVP, but we should add an exposure event in phase 2.

Sean Gallagher: and please define kill criteria. If error rate for progress svc > X or console p95 increases by Y, we flip to 0%.

Connor O'Brien: yep will add go/no-go + kill switch thresholds in rollout plan.

Marissa Cole: thanks all. once config is updated, ping me and I’ll sanity check in staging.

Dylan Brooks: will do. :thumbsup:

Logan Wright: for accurate funnel denominators, explicit exposure event is nice. but we can ship MVP with snapshot and add event later.

Marissa Cole: I’m ok with snapshot for MVP as long as we can answer “who saw the checklist” reliably. Support will ask.

Amara Diallo: question: if we don’t gate gateway event emission, do we risk “step complete” happening for tenants who never saw checklist? ex: api key created, first 2xx request, etc.

Chloe Martin: that’s fine. progress state is basically computed from behavior; showing UI is separate. if tenant later gets exposed, their checklist will appear partially complete (which might be good!).

Ben Carter: agree. and it reduces “cold start” confusion.

Nikhil Sharma: BUT: need to make sure step ordering doesn’t look weird if they already did prod stuff. e.g., “Set budget alerts” might be incomplete while “Send production traffic” looks complete.

Dylan Brooks: UX spec has “Recommended next” behavior. If some advanced steps are complete, we still show sequence but highlight the first incomplete. Also can collapse completed steps.

Talia Benson: ok. rollout mechanics: what cohorts for phase 1 dogfood?

Connor O'Brien: proposal:
- Week 1 internal (Redwood tenants) + 3 handpicked partner tenants
- Then 5% new self-serve signups (created_at >= rollout date)
- Then 25% all eligible
Need to decide if we only target *new* tenants or include existing.

Ben Carter: for activation lift we care mainly new. I’d do new-only initially to avoid surprising existing customers in Console. But telemetry wants some existing for baseline comparisons.

Logan Wright: baseline comparisons don’t require exposure; we can compare pre vs post on new signups only. I’m ok with new-only for the UI.

Marissa Cole: new-only simplifies Support training too (“If they signed up after X they probably have it”).

Sean Gallagher: new-only reduces blast radius. do it.

Chloe Martin: implementing “new-only” requires we have tenant.created_at in config service. do we already have that accessible?

Ethan Park: yes in control plane. config svc can evaluate rule `created_at >= 2025-02-xx`.

Dylan Brooks: one nuance: if we gate on created_at, tenants created just before date never get it unless we run a migration/override. maybe we want an allowlist for “requested access”.

Connor O'Brien: we can include an override list. Support asked for this anyway (ENG-5852 admin override is separate though). For rollout gating, we can maintain a “rollout allowlist” in config.

Ben Carter: yes. allowlist should be in control plane, editable by oncall/Release coord.

Allison Grant: please put guardrails: any manual allowlist change should be audited. doesn’t need full SOC2 today but at least who/when.

Ethan Park: we can log changes in config admin logs.

LaunchDarkly Bot: LaunchDarkly flag update: `console.onboarding_checklist` created in project `redwood-console`. Default: off. Rules: allowlist (internal tenants), then 0% rollout.

Dylan Brooks: wait we’re using LaunchDarkly directly? I thought we were keeping feature flags in control plane config.

Connor O'Brien: sorry, to clarify: pr-28467 uses LD SDK server-side in config service. Console reads config service, not LD directly.

Chloe Martin: ok that’s fine.

Nadia Rahman: if LD is used, please ensure config service caches evaluation results (per tenant) w/ TTL. don’t want every console page load hammering LD.

Ethan Park: caching is in place. We cache per tenant_id for 5 minutes; bust cache on config change webhook.

Sean Gallagher: 5 minutes ok. make sure kill switch propagation meets SRE ask. webhook path must be reliable.

Ethan Park: webhook already used for other flags. if webhook fails, worst case TTL 5 min.

Connor O'Brien: decision points to record:
1) tenant-level bucketing
2) config service evaluates flag (LD backed)
3) UI gated, event emission not gated
4) new tenants only initially + allowlist override
5) fail closed if progress svc down (hide component)
Anything else?

Marissa Cole: also: exclude enterprise contracts at first? I’d exclude anything with `plan_tier=enterprise` until Support + CS trained.

Ben Carter: I’d exclude enterprise by default, but allowlist for design partners.

Connor O'Brien: ok. Ethan can add rule in config service.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll update Console to display a small “Beta” pill only for dogfood cohort? or for all. UX spec says avoid “beta” unless we think it’s unstable.

Nikhil Sharma: avoid beta label; it makes customers nervous. use normal UI.

Talia Benson: timeline: can we land the rule changes today so we can start dogfood tomorrow?

Ethan Park: yes if decisions final. I’ll update config rules + add fields returned to Console: `enabled`, `cohort`, `rollout_reason` (for debugging; not shown in UI).

Allison Grant: careful with `rollout_reason` surfacing to client. don’t leak internal segmentation logic. keep it server logs only.

Dylan Brooks: +1. we can keep it behind an internal header or only in logs.

Connor O'Brien: ok. action items:
- Ethan: implement new-only + enterprise exclude + allowlist; keep reason server-side
- Dylan: ensure progress API calls are gated + timeout/fail closed + no retries
- Logan/Nadia: confirm telemetry approach for exposure denominator (snapshot ok MVP)
- Connor: update PM-768 + rollout doc with these decisions

Logan Wright: I’ll post in #eng-platform that telemetry is ok with snapshot for MVP, but we should add an exposure event in phase 2.

Sean Gallagher: and please define kill criteria. If error rate for progress svc > X or console p95 increases by Y, we flip to 0%.

Connor O'Brien: yep will add go/no-go + kill switch thresholds in rollout plan.

Marissa Cole: thanks all. once config is updated, ping me and I’ll sanity check in staging.

Dylan Brooks: will do. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-ml: 1738885126
preview:
Selene Huang: trying to pin down what we mean by a “quality regression alert” for route experiments. we need something we can defend + implement w/o full stat-sig in GA. can we align on trigger conditions + how it shows up in Console?

Jonas Weber: +1. we have eval score streams per {experiment_id, variant_id} from eval harness, but we need: (a) baseline definition (control?) (b) normalization (scores across suites) (c) min sample size / windowing.

Olivia Grant: reminder: the current hook gives us per-prompt outputs w/ a score vector (one per eval rubric) + an overall “quality_index” we can compute. but “overall” is still a product choice.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: my preference: make “quality regression” conservative + explainable. no p-values. do thresholding on a normalized aggregate + require minimum coverage. also avoid calling it “bad model” — it’s “quality signal suggests regression”.

Marissa Cole: from product/Console perspective we need something that fits the guardrails pattern next to latency/error/cost. so: a single status (healthy/degraded) + details drill-down.

Logan Wright: telemetry side: we can carry dimensions, but please keep cardinality bounded. if “quality” is a separate pipeline, the Console just needs: current value, delta vs control, sample count, last updated.

Selene Huang: ok, proposal v0:
- define control variant = variant with allocation “control=true” (or first variant if not set)
- compute Quality Score = quality_index normalized to 0-100 per eval suite, then aggregate as weighted average (default weights = 1 unless specified)
- alert triggers if delta_vs_control <= -X AND sample_count >= N AND eval_coverage >= C%
question: pick X/N/C.

Jonas Weber: control flag is good. for normalization: we can do per-suite z-score then map to 0-100, but that’s hard to explain. would rather keep it simple:
- quality_index already in [0,1] in harness output for most rubrics
- we can scale to [0,100]
- if a suite is “lower is better” we invert in harness once.

Olivia Grant: fwiw harness PR-812 output already includes a `higher_is_better` bit + we can standardize direction. so Console sees comparable numbers.

Alicia Perez: UX note: we need to show when we *don’t know*. e.g. “Insufficient eval data” state. otherwise customers will interpret tiny blips as regression.

Rafael Mendes: from SRE/guardrails: also need to define severity + whether it triggers auto-stop (probably not by default). we already agreed initial guardrails are soft-stop. quality should be “notify + banner” only.

Marissa Cole: yes. GA promise: quality guardrail exists, but it does NOT auto-stop unless customer explicitly enables (future). for now it contributes to “Experiment health: Degraded” with reason.

Selene Huang: +1. so for trigger values: I’m thinking:
- N = at least 200 eval’d requests per variant in the last 6h OR 50 in last 1h (whichever first)
- C = at least 80% of the configured prompt set completed in window
- X = drop of >= 3.0 points on 0-100 scale (absolute), OR >= 5% relative drop if baseline is low?

Jonas Weber: careful w/ absolute threshold only. if baseline is 95, -3 is meaningful. if baseline is 60, -3 could be noise. but we’re explicitly not doing significance. maybe combine absolute + relative:
Trigger if (delta_abs <= -3) AND (delta_rel <= -0.03)  (i.e., at least 3 points and 3% down).

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: agree w/ combined gating. also add a “cooldown” / “two consecutive windows” requirement to reduce flapping.

Logan Wright: windowing is important. if you do “two consecutive windows” pick a fixed interval (e.g., 30m). telemetry aggregation can support that.

Olivia Grant: note eval sampling isn’t 100% of traffic. for small allocations we might not hit N quickly. so we need the “insufficient data” case and *don’t* show a red badge prematurely.

Alicia Perez: can we model it like:
- Quality: Healthy (green)
- Quality: Degraded (red)
- Quality: Insufficient data (gray)
- Quality: Not configured (gray)
and tooltips that explain exactly what’s required.

Marissa Cole: yes. and in results view: show a mini card “Quality vs Control” with sparkline, delta, sample count, and “last eval run” timestamp.

Selene Huang: what about multiple eval suites? customers may attach different prompt sets to variants.

Jonas Weber: for v1, I think we require the prompt set is configured at experiment-level (same for all variants) to keep comparability. if variant-specific prompt sets, we should mark as “non-comparable” and not compute delta. (or compute but scary).

Marissa Cole: PRD assumes same eval suite per experiment for now, I’m ok to codify that in validation.

Rafael Mendes: +1. comparable signals only. otherwise support will get wrecked.

Selene Huang: ok, draft definition:
Quality regression alert (Console guardrail) triggers when:
1) Experiment has eval suite configured (prompt set + scoring config) and is comparable across variants.
2) For a variant, within the evaluation window (rolling 6h, refreshed every 30m):
   - eval_sample_count >= 200 (or >= 50 in last 1h for early warning)
   - coverage >= 80% of prompt set (or “coverage >= 40%” for early warning?)
3) Variant’s aggregated Quality Score delta vs control meets BOTH:
   - absolute drop >= 3 points (0-100)
   - relative drop >= 3%
4) Condition holds for 2 consecutive evaluation ticks (to avoid flapping)
Action: mark guardrail as Degraded, emit notification event, but do NOT auto-stop.

Jonas Weber: I’d avoid the 40% coverage early warning, it’s going to be noisy. maybe do early warning solely on sample count (>=50) but still require coverage >=80%. otherwise a few easy prompts might bias the score.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: agree. coverage requirement is about representativeness. keep it.

Logan Wright: implementation detail: store both sample_count and prompt_count_completed to compute coverage. also store baseline score + variant score so Console can show numbers.

Alicia Perez: wording suggestion for UI:
- “Quality Degraded”
- Subtitle: “Quality score decreased by 4.2 (4.5%) vs control over last 6h (n=312 evals).”
- Secondary: “Eval coverage: 92% of prompt set. Updated 12m ago.”

Marissa Cole: love that. also add a “View details” -> table with per-suite deltas so people can see *why*.

Olivia Grant: per-suite deltas are feasible: e.g. `helpfulness`, `factuality`, `format_adherence`. just keep the default view on the aggregate.

Rafael Mendes: for guardrail thresholds: make X and relative threshold configurable? or hard-coded for beta?

Marissa Cole: beta: hard-coded defaults, maybe behind “advanced settings” later. customers already have too many knobs.

Selene Huang: ok. defaults baked for beta/GA v1, but we expose the thresholds in docs.

Jonas Weber: one more constraint: control score has to be “stable enough” too. if control n is tiny, delta is meaningless. so apply N/coverage gating to BOTH control + variant before computing delta.

Selene Huang: good catch. add that.

Logan Wright: +1. also we should version the definition so if thresholds change later we can annotate historical alerts.

Marissa Cole: action items:
- @Selene Huang write up this definition in the quality guardrails doc + send to me for UX copy
- @Olivia Grant confirm harness provides higher_is_better + suite-level outputs consistently
- @Logan Wright ensure results API can carry fields: aggregate_score, delta_abs, delta_rel, n, coverage, window_start/end, last_updated

Selene Huang: will do. I’ll add explicit “Insufficient data” state when gating not met (n < N or coverage < 80%).

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: :thumbsup: and please add a note: “not a statistical significance test” and “interpret with context”. we should be transparent.

Alicia Perez: :+1: I’ll mock the guardrail card w/ the copy above and include the 3-state status.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. once this is in, SRE can decide if/when we page on quality (probably never) but we can route to Slack notifications channel.

Selene Huang: cool. I’ll post a draft in confluence later today and link it here.
