title: #eng-platform: 1738876422
preview:
Connor O'Brien: Kicking off coordination thread for tier→pool mapping rollout (feature-flagged). Goal: ship safely w/ guardrails + canary plan. Current PR is `pr-19366-routing-tier-to-pool-selector` behind `routing.tier_pool_mapping`. Need alignment on: default behavior, fallback rules, canary accounts, and what dashboards/alerts we watch.

Mina Haddad: thx. top concern: we can’t route Premium traffic to a pool that doesn’t have headroom and accidentally create a self-inflicted SLO miss. also need deterministic behavior when mapping is missing / stale.

Ravi Menon: +1. I want to avoid “surprise” routing changes for existing Dedicated deployments. Would prefer:
- Phase 0: compute mapping + emit decision logs/metrics (shadow)
- Phase 1: enforce for allowlisted deployments only
- Phase 2: roll out tier-by-tier

Elena Popov: Implementation detail: selector currently does:
1) look up `tier` from deployment config
2) look up `tier_pool_map[region][tier]` (config service)
3) pick pool w/ least saturation among candidates
If no match, it falls back to `existing_pool` (what deployment is pinned to today).

Ethan Park: That fallback sounds right. We should not “invent” a new pool if tier config is incomplete. Also for control plane: we can version `tier_pool_map` and attach `tier_pool_map_version` to routing decisions so we can audit changes.

Keiko Yamamoto: How does this interact w/ admission control? If we route more premium to premium pool, we still need the premium pool to shed overload *before* it drags tokens/sec down. Tier-aware admission control PR (`pr-19348`) is ready-ish but we’re still tuning queue caps.

Mina Haddad: In my head: routing chooses target pool, then admission control enforces per-tier queue/latency bounds. For Dedicated, if premium pool is saturated, we should reject/queue (depending on tier) rather than silently route to Standard.

Rafael Mendes: From incident posture: silent downgrade routing is worst because customer sees quality/latency change and we can’t explain. Prefer explicit failure mode with a clean error + we page if it threatens SLO.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. From SLO semantics side: throughput SLO assumes traffic goes to the committed capacity. If we spill to other pool(s), measurement becomes weird. Let’s keep “spillover” as an explicit opt-in and meter it.

Connor O'Brien: OK so fallback order proposal:
- If tier mapping exists and pool is healthy: route there.
- If tier mapping exists but pool is unhealthy/saturated: do NOT reroute to different tier automatically. Use admission control behavior (shed/429 or queue) per tier policy.
- If tier mapping missing: fallback to existing_pool.
- If existing_pool missing (shouldn’t happen): fallback to default pool AND emit a high-sev alert.

Cole Summers: +1 on metering. If we ever add spillover/burst pool routing, we need to tag events as `committed` vs `burst` anyway (ENG-19302). For now, if we’re shedding, ensure the response includes `x-redwood-overload: true` and tier in headers so CS can debug.

Logan Wright: Telemetry: we can add a counter like `router_tier_pool_decision_total{tier, region, result}` where result in [mapped, missing_map_fallback_existing, unhealthy_admission_control, missing_everything_default]. Also histograms for time in selector would be good.

Sergio Costa: For dashboards, I can add panels:
- % of requests by tier routed to expected pool (mapped)
- saturation per pool + tier overlay
- top deployments hitting `unhealthy_admission_control`
Need to know canary deployment_ids to build quick filters.

Connor O'Brien: Canary plan draft:
0) SHADOW in prod: enable `routing.tier_pool_mapping.shadow=true` (compute + emit metrics only) for all, 24h.
1) ENFORCE for internal test deployments (Eng sandbox + demo env), 2h.
2) ENFORCE for 3 beta customers (from Product list): BluePeak (Plus), Vidorama (Plus), HyperDoc (Premium) in us-east only.
3) Expand to 25% of Dedicated deployments (randomized allowlist) in us-east + eu-west.
4) Full rollout.

Mina Haddad: I’m good w/ that but step 2 needs gating on pool capacity. Infra is still uneven in eu-west premium pool.

Elena Popov: Flag knobs we have today:
- `routing.tier_pool_mapping.enabled` (enforce)
- `routing.tier_pool_mapping.shadow` (log-only)
- `routing.tier_pool_mapping.allowlist_deployments` (csv)
- `routing.tier_pool_mapping.regions` (optional)
We can do us-east only initially.

Keiko Yamamoto: Can we also add an auto-kill switch? e.g. if `router_mapping_missing` spikes above X% or admission control shed rate spikes, flip flag off.

Rafael Mendes: We can handle via oncall playbook + quick toggle, but an automatic rollback on bad signals is nice. If it’s too much code, at least define thresholds + page.

Sean Gallagher: For thresholds: if mapped traffic drops below 99% for allowlisted deployments OR if `unhealthy_admission_control` > 0.5% sustained 10m, page. (These are early numbers, we’ll tune.)

Sergio Costa: Works. I’ll wire alert suggestions but need final thresholds from SRE.

Connor O'Brien: I’ll sync w/ Rafael/Sean for burn-rate vs router alerts. For rollout, we’ll do this as a release checklist item w/ feature flag changes.

Ethan Park: One more thing: config propagation delay. Tier map updates can take ~60s to reach all routers. During that, we might get mixed decisions. That’s fine if fallback is existing_pool, but we should avoid “flapping” between two mapped pools if map changes.

Ravi Menon: Can we pin mapping per deployment? Like `deployment.pool_override` set during provisioning for now, and tier mapping only affects new deployments until we’re confident.

Mina Haddad: That’s actually what I want: existing Dedicated should remain pinned unless we explicitly migrate. Tier mapping should be used for (a) new provisioning and (b) opt-in migration.

Elena Popov: We can support that with a simple rule:
- If `deployment.pool_override` present => always use it.
- Else => apply tier mapping.
Currently “existing_pool” is effectively that override.

Connor O'Brien: Great. Let’s document as: “tier mapping applies only when deployment is not pinned.”

Cole Summers: For CS/Sales, pinned vs mapped needs to be visible somewhere. Can we at least emit it in the deployment metadata endpoint? (Even if Console UI comes later.)

Ethan Park: +1. We can add `routing_policy_source` in the deployment read API: `pinned|tier_map|default`.

Sergio Costa: If it’s in API, I can use it in dashboard filters. Console later.

Rafael Mendes: Rollout comms: please post in #eng-oncall when shadow begins and again when enforce begins. Oncall needs to know what changed.

Connor O'Brien: Will do.

DeployBot: Deployment scheduled by Connor O'Brien: `router-service` canary to us-east (shadow mode only). ETA 20m. (build: 7f3c1a2)

Keiko Yamamoto: For step 2 (beta customers), do we have their deployment_ids handy? Also confirm we’re not touching multi-region failover routing yet.

Connor O'Brien: Not touching failover routing in this change. This is primary pool selection only. For deployment_ids, I’ll grab from control plane and paste once confirmed.

Mina Haddad: Also request: for Premium, we must ensure premium pool has warm spares before we canary HyperDoc. If not, keep them out of canary.

Sean Gallagher: +1. I don’t want first enforcement to coincide with a capacity crunch.

Connor O'Brien: Action items:
- Connor: update rollout doc + release checklist; start shadow in us-east only.
- Elena: add `routing_policy_source` and `tier_pool_map_version` tags to decision logs.
- Sergio: prep dashboard panels + filters; draft alert queries.
- Rafael/Sean: propose initial alert thresholds + paging route.
- Mina/Keiko: confirm premium pool readiness for HyperDoc canary.

Ravi Menon: Sounds good. One more ask: please include a one-liner in the runbook for “how to disable” (flag + config) and “how to verify” (dashboard + log line). I’ve seen this get messy during incidents.

Connor O'Brien: Yup, I’ll add to `tier-aware-routing-and-overload-protection` runbook section.

Sergio Costa: Once I have deployment_ids, I’ll build a saved view for each canary account.

Rafael Mendes: ok. if we keep it us-east and pinned-first, risk is manageable. :thumbsup:

Connor O'Brien: Cool. I’ll post an update once shadow has been running 2h and metrics look sane.
text:
Connor O'Brien: Kicking off coordination thread for tier→pool mapping rollout (feature-flagged). Goal: ship safely w/ guardrails + canary plan. Current PR is `pr-19366-routing-tier-to-pool-selector` behind `routing.tier_pool_mapping`. Need alignment on: default behavior, fallback rules, canary accounts, and what dashboards/alerts we watch.

Mina Haddad: thx. top concern: we can’t route Premium traffic to a pool that doesn’t have headroom and accidentally create a self-inflicted SLO miss. also need deterministic behavior when mapping is missing / stale.

Ravi Menon: +1. I want to avoid “surprise” routing changes for existing Dedicated deployments. Would prefer:
- Phase 0: compute mapping + emit decision logs/metrics (shadow)
- Phase 1: enforce for allowlisted deployments only
- Phase 2: roll out tier-by-tier

Elena Popov: Implementation detail: selector currently does:
1) look up `tier` from deployment config
2) look up `tier_pool_map[region][tier]` (config service)
3) pick pool w/ least saturation among candidates
If no match, it falls back to `existing_pool` (what deployment is pinned to today).

Ethan Park: That fallback sounds right. We should not “invent” a new pool if tier config is incomplete. Also for control plane: we can version `tier_pool_map` and attach `tier_pool_map_version` to routing decisions so we can audit changes.

Keiko Yamamoto: How does this interact w/ admission control? If we route more premium to premium pool, we still need the premium pool to shed overload *before* it drags tokens/sec down. Tier-aware admission control PR (`pr-19348`) is ready-ish but we’re still tuning queue caps.

Mina Haddad: In my head: routing chooses target pool, then admission control enforces per-tier queue/latency bounds. For Dedicated, if premium pool is saturated, we should reject/queue (depending on tier) rather than silently route to Standard.

Rafael Mendes: From incident posture: silent downgrade routing is worst because customer sees quality/latency change and we can’t explain. Prefer explicit failure mode with a clean error + we page if it threatens SLO.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. From SLO semantics side: throughput SLO assumes traffic goes to the committed capacity. If we spill to other pool(s), measurement becomes weird. Let’s keep “spillover” as an explicit opt-in and meter it.

Connor O'Brien: OK so fallback order proposal:
- If tier mapping exists and pool is healthy: route there.
- If tier mapping exists but pool is unhealthy/saturated: do NOT reroute to different tier automatically. Use admission control behavior (shed/429 or queue) per tier policy.
- If tier mapping missing: fallback to existing_pool.
- If existing_pool missing (shouldn’t happen): fallback to default pool AND emit a high-sev alert.

Cole Summers: +1 on metering. If we ever add spillover/burst pool routing, we need to tag events as `committed` vs `burst` anyway (ENG-19302). For now, if we’re shedding, ensure the response includes `x-redwood-overload: true` and tier in headers so CS can debug.

Logan Wright: Telemetry: we can add a counter like `router_tier_pool_decision_total{tier, region, result}` where result in [mapped, missing_map_fallback_existing, unhealthy_admission_control, missing_everything_default]. Also histograms for time in selector would be good.

Sergio Costa: For dashboards, I can add panels:
- % of requests by tier routed to expected pool (mapped)
- saturation per pool + tier overlay
- top deployments hitting `unhealthy_admission_control`
Need to know canary deployment_ids to build quick filters.

Connor O'Brien: Canary plan draft:
0) SHADOW in prod: enable `routing.tier_pool_mapping.shadow=true` (compute + emit metrics only) for all, 24h.
1) ENFORCE for internal test deployments (Eng sandbox + demo env), 2h.
2) ENFORCE for 3 beta customers (from Product list): BluePeak (Plus), Vidorama (Plus), HyperDoc (Premium) in us-east only.
3) Expand to 25% of Dedicated deployments (randomized allowlist) in us-east + eu-west.
4) Full rollout.

Mina Haddad: I’m good w/ that but step 2 needs gating on pool capacity. Infra is still uneven in eu-west premium pool.

Elena Popov: Flag knobs we have today:
- `routing.tier_pool_mapping.enabled` (enforce)
- `routing.tier_pool_mapping.shadow` (log-only)
- `routing.tier_pool_mapping.allowlist_deployments` (csv)
- `routing.tier_pool_mapping.regions` (optional)
We can do us-east only initially.

Keiko Yamamoto: Can we also add an auto-kill switch? e.g. if `router_mapping_missing` spikes above X% or admission control shed rate spikes, flip flag off.

Rafael Mendes: We can handle via oncall playbook + quick toggle, but an automatic rollback on bad signals is nice. If it’s too much code, at least define thresholds + page.

Sean Gallagher: For thresholds: if mapped traffic drops below 99% for allowlisted deployments OR if `unhealthy_admission_control` > 0.5% sustained 10m, page. (These are early numbers, we’ll tune.)

Sergio Costa: Works. I’ll wire alert suggestions but need final thresholds from SRE.

Connor O'Brien: I’ll sync w/ Rafael/Sean for burn-rate vs router alerts. For rollout, we’ll do this as a release checklist item w/ feature flag changes.

Ethan Park: One more thing: config propagation delay. Tier map updates can take ~60s to reach all routers. During that, we might get mixed decisions. That’s fine if fallback is existing_pool, but we should avoid “flapping” between two mapped pools if map changes.

Ravi Menon: Can we pin mapping per deployment? Like `deployment.pool_override` set during provisioning for now, and tier mapping only affects new deployments until we’re confident.

Mina Haddad: That’s actually what I want: existing Dedicated should remain pinned unless we explicitly migrate. Tier mapping should be used for (a) new provisioning and (b) opt-in migration.

Elena Popov: We can support that with a simple rule:
- If `deployment.pool_override` present => always use it.
- Else => apply tier mapping.
Currently “existing_pool” is effectively that override.

Connor O'Brien: Great. Let’s document as: “tier mapping applies only when deployment is not pinned.”

Cole Summers: For CS/Sales, pinned vs mapped needs to be visible somewhere. Can we at least emit it in the deployment metadata endpoint? (Even if Console UI comes later.)

Ethan Park: +1. We can add `routing_policy_source` in the deployment read API: `pinned|tier_map|default`.

Sergio Costa: If it’s in API, I can use it in dashboard filters. Console later.

Rafael Mendes: Rollout comms: please post in #eng-oncall when shadow begins and again when enforce begins. Oncall needs to know what changed.

Connor O'Brien: Will do.

DeployBot: Deployment scheduled by Connor O'Brien: `router-service` canary to us-east (shadow mode only). ETA 20m. (build: 7f3c1a2)

Keiko Yamamoto: For step 2 (beta customers), do we have their deployment_ids handy? Also confirm we’re not touching multi-region failover routing yet.

Connor O'Brien: Not touching failover routing in this change. This is primary pool selection only. For deployment_ids, I’ll grab from control plane and paste once confirmed.

Mina Haddad: Also request: for Premium, we must ensure premium pool has warm spares before we canary HyperDoc. If not, keep them out of canary.

Sean Gallagher: +1. I don’t want first enforcement to coincide with a capacity crunch.

Connor O'Brien: Action items:
- Connor: update rollout doc + release checklist; start shadow in us-east only.
- Elena: add `routing_policy_source` and `tier_pool_map_version` tags to decision logs.
- Sergio: prep dashboard panels + filters; draft alert queries.
- Rafael/Sean: propose initial alert thresholds + paging route.
- Mina/Keiko: confirm premium pool readiness for HyperDoc canary.

Ravi Menon: Sounds good. One more ask: please include a one-liner in the runbook for “how to disable” (flag + config) and “how to verify” (dashboard + log line). I’ve seen this get messy during incidents.

Connor O'Brien: Yup, I’ll add to `tier-aware-routing-and-overload-protection` runbook section.

Sergio Costa: Once I have deployment_ids, I’ll build a saved view for each canary account.

Rafael Mendes: ok. if we keep it us-east and pinned-first, risk is manageable. :thumbsup:

Connor O'Brien: Cool. I’ll post an update once shadow has been running 2h and metrics look sane.

title: #devex: 1738874409
preview:
Monica Patel: dropping the SDK “production defaults” proposal here for review. TL;DR: we’ve got too many first-prod launch issues that are basically missing timeouts/retries + weird streaming disconnect behavior. Proposal is to ship sane presets across Python/TS/Go and update quickstarts/snippets to use them. Question: do we make these actual *defaults* or do we keep current behavior and expose an opt-in preset? Biggest worry is breaking folks who rely on “infinite” requests or who are doing their own retries.

Monica Patel: draft defaults (if we went that route):
- non-streaming: connect timeout 5s, request timeout 60s, retries: 2 (only on 408/409/429/5xx), backoff w/ jitter, respect `Retry-After`
- streaming: connect timeout 5s, no overall request timeout (or very high), but heartbeat / read timeout 30s, and reconnect helper (no auto-retry by default)
- also: emit `User-Agent: redwood-sdk/<ver> preset=prod` tag so Telemetry can attribute funnel

Ibrahim Khan: +1 on presets, -1 on silently changing defaults. Any “retry by default” in SDK land becomes a support magnet when customers see duplicate side effects (even if most endpoints are idempotent-ish).

Ibrahim Khan: for chat/generate, server side *should* be idempotent given same input, but customers sometimes include things like tool calls that trigger external actions. we can’t assume “safe to retry” at the client.

Brian Murphy: agree. also OpenAI-compat users will assume OpenAI semantics (no automatic retries unless they add them). If we change defaults it’ll be a “why did you retry this?” surprise.

Dana Lo: on TS specifically: implementing a hard request timeout by default is risky because `fetch` + streaming is weird. If we set a global `AbortController` deadline, we’ll kill long-lived streams and people will file “streaming randomly stops at 60s.”

Dana Lo: I’d rather provide helpers:
- `client = new Redwood({ retry: RedwoodRetry.production(), timeout: RedwoodTimeouts.production() })`
- and a separate `stream()` helper that supports reconnect logic with an explicit opt-in.

Wei Chen: from docs side, I’m fine with “recommended defaults” / presets and updating quickstart snippets to include them. That still moves TTFS/TTFP without us taking on backward compatibility risk.

Wei Chen: also: we should be super explicit in docs that streaming needs different handling. We keep seeing folks run streaming from local scripts with no keepalive and then they think the model is broken.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go thoughts: we should not hide deadlines. idiomatic Go is context-based timeouts. If we change defaults we’ll fight Go expectations.

Mateusz Zielinski: proposal: in go sdk, keep the core client “dumb”, but ship:
- `redwood.ProductionClient()` that sets transport timeouts + retry policy
- `StreamWithReconnect(ctx, req, opts)` helper

Mateusz Zielinski: also note: if we retry on 429 but the caller is already retrying, it doubles. docs should include “disable built-in retries if you already have a retry layer.”

Logan Wright: +1 to the `User-Agent` tagging, but we need to make sure it doesn’t explode cardinality. `preset=prod` is fine. please no dynamic values (like timeout ms) in UA.

Nadia Rahman: re: respect `Retry-After`: we need to be careful. sometimes gateway sends `Retry-After: 0` / missing header depending on the path. SDK should have sane fallback backoff.

Nadia Rahman: also we should align with what gateway actually returns for 429 today (some endpoints use `x-ratelimit-reset-ms`). If we can’t guarantee headers, SDK behavior should be documented (and not “magical”).

Sean Gallagher: SRE hat: I’m nervous about defaults that can amplify traffic during an incident. Even “2 retries” can turn into a thundering herd if there’s a partial outage.

Sean Gallagher: if we do built-in retries, please cap by total retry time and add jitter. And ideally have a global kill switch via env var (or config) so support can ask customers to disable quickly.

Sabrina James: quickstarts/snippets angle: if presets exist but aren’t used by default, we *must* update the snippet generator to use `preset=production` in all languages. otherwise we’ll keep teaching unsafe usage.

Sabrina James: also, should the preset enable structured logging hooks? at least request id + status + latency (but obviously avoid prompt content).

Elliot Price: docs IA: if we don’t change defaults, we should avoid wording like “the SDK retries by default.” It should be “enable retries with the production preset” + show how to tune.

Monica Patel: ok sounds like consensus is “opt-in preset”, not changing defaults globally. what about making it the default only for *new API keys / new projects* via Console? (ex: copy/paste snippet includes it)

Brian Murphy: that’s the right place to enforce. Console snippet + docs + checklist step == user chooses it.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. We can also add a warning in Console if they haven’t configured any timeout/retry and they’re about to set a webhook/prod traffic. (not MVP but later)

Dana Lo: TS detail: for streaming, I’m strongly against auto-reconnect “by default.” But I’m good with `client.chat.streamWithReconnect()` that’s clearly labeled and requires an idempotency key or at least warns.

Mateusz Zielinski: same for Go. helpers, not magic.

Logan Wright: for onboarding funnel, we can also treat “production preset enabled” as a checklist step completion event. Emitted server-side via UA + request headers.

Nadia Rahman: +1, but make sure we don’t treat it as “completed” if they just installed SDK. should be on first successful 2xx with UA tag.

Monica Patel: action items I’m hearing:
1) Ship `production` presets for timeouts/retries in Python/TS/Go (opt-in)
2) Update snippet generator + quickstarts to use preset by default
3) Add `User-Agent` tag `preset=production` (static) for attribution
4) Define retry policy precisely (only 408/409/429/5xx; never on 4xx other than 408/409/429; respect headers when present; cap total retry time; jitter)
5) Streaming: provide reconnect helper but keep off by default; add docs on heartbeat/read timeout expectations

Monica Patel: if no objections, I’ll update the doc + open the Linear tickets under ENG-5820 for implementation details. please drop any edge cases we’re missing.

Sean Gallagher: one more: make sure retries don’t retry *after* a request has started streaming tokens. that can create weird partial responses.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. once bytes are received, don’t retry. also should surface “retry_count” in logs so customers can see what happened.

Dana Lo: ack. I’ll take a stab at TS API shape and post in-thread later today.

Sabrina James: I’ll sync with Wei on snippet updates. Once preset names are final, we can update quickstarts + templates.

Monica Patel: perfect, thanks all. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-platform: 1738798031
preview:
Felix Schneider: Heads up: seeing a pretty gnarly DB load spike tied to Console route experiment *results* view. :warning: Grafana panel for `console-api postgres cpu` jumped from ~35% -> 92% when a couple folks opened Results dashboards.

Felix Schneider: Looks like the new “variant deltas” endpoint is doing a heavy query when you toggle `group_by=model` / `group_by=region`. Anyone around to help triage? I can drop query sample.

Chloe Martin: yep, looking. Which env/cluster? prod or staging?

Felix Schneider: prod. `console-api` hitting `cp-prod-aurora-01`. Started ~10 min ago.

Deploy Bot: [alert] aurora-cp-prod-aurora-01: CPUUtilization > 85% for 10m (sev2)

Sean Gallagher: on it. Can we confirm it’s *read* saturation vs locks?

Felix Schneider: from RDS insights: mostly read + temp file usage. no obvious lock waits.

Chloe Martin: paste query / explain if you have it.

Felix Schneider: from slow query log (redacted ids):
```
SELECT
  date_trunc('minute', bucket_ts) as t,
  variant_id,
  region,
  model_id,
  percentile_cont(0.95) within group (order by latency_ms) as p95,
  sum(prompt_tokens + completion_tokens) as tokens,
  sum(cost_usd) as cost
FROM experiment_request_facts
WHERE experiment_id = $1
  AND route_id = $2
  AND bucket_ts BETWEEN $3 AND $4
GROUP BY 1,2,3,4
ORDER BY 1 ASC;
```
This is for `group_by=region,model` (the toggle in UI).

Allison Grant: that’s going straight to request_facts?? that table is huge, right? we intended results UI to hit the rollups.

Logan Wright: yeah, request_facts is the “raw-ish” attribution table. Console should be reading `experiment_variant_metrics_hourly` (or 5m) rollup dataset.

Mei Lin: +1. GROUP BY 4 dims over a large time range = temp files + pain. Also `bucket_ts BETWEEN` on a non-partitioned table will scan more than you think.

Chloe Martin: I think the new Results endpoint is using a fallback code path when rollups aren’t available (it tries to be “real time” if aggregation lags). Might be kicking in due to rollup freshness check.

Nadia Rahman: also cost_usd in request_facts is derived from token accounting join, right? if that join is happening inline anywhere, it’s worse.

Felix Schneider: the endpoint is `/v1/experiments/:id/results` (from PR-28644/28658). When you open Results it requests last 24h by default.

Jordan Lee: can we just cap it to 1h until we fix? 24h *by minute* *and* grouping by region/model is asking for it.

Dylan Brooks: jumping in. We need an immediate mitigation (today) + proper fix (rollups/caching). What’s quickest? feature-flag disable the group_by toggles?

Chloe Martin: quick mitigations I can ship:
1) hard cap default window to last 1h (still allow 24h via explicit “Load more”)
2) disallow `group_by=region,model` combined for now (pick one)
3) add response cache (in-memory) keyed by `{experiment_id, route_id, window, group_by}` with 60s TTL

Sean Gallagher: do (1) + (2) now. Cache is good but doesn’t fix first-hit thundering herd if a bunch of ppl click the same dashboard after a deploy.

Allison Grant: what about server-side “top-N regions/models + other”? we don’t need all combos.

Logan Wright: longer-term we should not be grouping raw request_facts at all. We already emit rollups by {route, experiment_id, variant_id, region, model_id} at 5m granularity in analytics. Console should query that.

Mei Lin: if we *must* query request_facts in emergency, add index on `(experiment_id, route_id, bucket_ts)` at minimum. But that’s still heavy for percentile calc.

Caleb Johnson: percentile_cont over millions of rows is expensive. Our rollup tables store precomputed p50/p95/p99 already.

Felix Schneider: why isn’t it using rollup? :thinking:

Chloe Martin: I found it. The endpoint checks “rollup staleness” and if lag > 10m it flips to raw to “avoid stale charts”. Our rollup lag monitor is currently reading ~18m in prod (aggregation job behind).

Rafael Mendes: ^ that matches what I saw earlier: the experiment rollup job is running slower after we added dimensions. Might be backlog from yesterday.

Nadia Rahman: yep cost attribution step got heavier after adding experiment tags. We can reduce by moving cost calc into the rollup job once per bucket (not per request query).

Dylan Brooks: so two parallel tracks:
- Console: stop falling back to raw (or make fallback *coarser* / smaller window)
- Analytics: fix rollup lag

Sean Gallagher: I’m strongly against “fallback to raw” in prod if it can melt the DB. Prefer stale data + banner over taking out control plane.

Allison Grant: agree. We can show “Data is delayed (~18m)” and keep dashboard responsive.

Chloe Martin: proposed change: remove raw fallback entirely for prod, behind `route_experiments_results_raw_fallback` flag default OFF. Only allow for internal staging.

Felix Schneider: +1. UX wise we can add a yellow banner “Results delayed” if rollup staleness > threshold.

Logan Wright: for rollup lag: I can check the aggregator workers. Might need to bump concurrency or reduce cardinality expansions (we had that thread last week).

Mei Lin: also query side: ensure results endpoint is hitting the read replica / analytics store if possible, not primary CP DB.

Caleb Johnson: the rollup dataset lives in analytics DB already. Console results API is still wired to CP DB because it was easiest. We should switch to analytics connection for results.

Dylan Brooks: ok, immediate plan:
1) Chloe: hotfix to disable raw fallback + cap default window to 1h + disable combined group_by (ship behind flag, roll out asap)
2) Logan/Caleb/Mei: unstick rollup lag (today) + confirm where rollup lives and best query path
3) Sean/Rafael: keep an eye on DB CPU, set temporary alert threshold + paging until mitigations are out

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: also add a rate limit / circuit breaker on that endpoint. If `db_cpu>80%`, return 429 with “try again” vs continuing to pile on.

Chloe Martin: I can add a simple guard: if request asks for > 6h window with minute granularity, reject. Also clamp bucket size to 5m/1h depending on window.

Felix Schneider: I’ll open a quick UI patch to make 24h not the default + hide region/model combo toggle until backend fix. (I’ll coordinate with Kenji later)

Rafael Mendes: meanwhile I’m muting non-critical alerts to reduce noise, but keeping the sev2 on CPU.

Deploy Bot: deploy started: console-api hotfix branch `disable-results-raw-fallback` -> prod canary

Logan Wright: update: rollup workers are behind due to a bad query plan on `experiment_variant_metrics_5m` upsert. Mei, I’m DMing you the job logs. Might need an index on conflict target.

Mei Lin: got it. The `ON CONFLICT (experiment_id, route_id, variant_id, bucket_ts, region, model_id)` needs a matching unique index; I don’t think it exists in prod.

Caleb Johnson: wow ok that would absolutely explain backlog.

Nadia Rahman: if we’re adding that unique index, please double-check cardinality constraints (region/model normalized) so we don’t explode row count.

Chloe Martin: canary is live. Raw fallback disabled, default window set to 1h. Combined group_by returns 400 with message “Select a single breakdown (region OR model)”.

Sean Gallagher: DB CPU already trending down: 92% -> 63% in last 5m.

Felix Schneider: dashboard is usable again on my end. Results are slightly delayed but at least page loads.

Allison Grant: please add a postmortem-ish note somewhere: “Never query raw request_facts for percentiles from Console.” This will come up again.

Dylan Brooks: +1. After incident: we should (a) move results to analytics DB (b) add caching (c) ensure rollup index exists via migration (d) keep stale-banner behavior.

Deploy Bot: deploy finished: console-api hotfix -> prod 100%

Rafael Mendes: marking incident mitigated. Keeping watch until rollup backlog is cleared.

Logan Wright: rollup index migration queued for tonight’s maintenance window. In the meantime we increased worker concurrency + reduced per-bucket cost calc.

Sean Gallagher: thanks all. If CPU spikes again, we’ll flip the results feature flag off for affected tenants.
text:
Felix Schneider: Heads up: seeing a pretty gnarly DB load spike tied to Console route experiment *results* view. :warning: Grafana panel for `console-api postgres cpu` jumped from ~35% -> 92% when a couple folks opened Results dashboards.

Felix Schneider: Looks like the new “variant deltas” endpoint is doing a heavy query when you toggle `group_by=model` / `group_by=region`. Anyone around to help triage? I can drop query sample.

Chloe Martin: yep, looking. Which env/cluster? prod or staging?

Felix Schneider: prod. `console-api` hitting `cp-prod-aurora-01`. Started ~10 min ago.

Deploy Bot: [alert] aurora-cp-prod-aurora-01: CPUUtilization > 85% for 10m (sev2)

Sean Gallagher: on it. Can we confirm it’s *read* saturation vs locks?

Felix Schneider: from RDS insights: mostly read + temp file usage. no obvious lock waits.

Chloe Martin: paste query / explain if you have it.

Felix Schneider: from slow query log (redacted ids):
```
SELECT
  date_trunc('minute', bucket_ts) as t,
  variant_id,
  region,
  model_id,
  percentile_cont(0.95) within group (order by latency_ms) as p95,
  sum(prompt_tokens + completion_tokens) as tokens,
  sum(cost_usd) as cost
FROM experiment_request_facts
WHERE experiment_id = $1
  AND route_id = $2
  AND bucket_ts BETWEEN $3 AND $4
GROUP BY 1,2,3,4
ORDER BY 1 ASC;
```
This is for `group_by=region,model` (the toggle in UI).

Allison Grant: that’s going straight to request_facts?? that table is huge, right? we intended results UI to hit the rollups.

Logan Wright: yeah, request_facts is the “raw-ish” attribution table. Console should be reading `experiment_variant_metrics_hourly` (or 5m) rollup dataset.

Mei Lin: +1. GROUP BY 4 dims over a large time range = temp files + pain. Also `bucket_ts BETWEEN` on a non-partitioned table will scan more than you think.

Chloe Martin: I think the new Results endpoint is using a fallback code path when rollups aren’t available (it tries to be “real time” if aggregation lags). Might be kicking in due to rollup freshness check.

Nadia Rahman: also cost_usd in request_facts is derived from token accounting join, right? if that join is happening inline anywhere, it’s worse.

Felix Schneider: the endpoint is `/v1/experiments/:id/results` (from PR-28644/28658). When you open Results it requests last 24h by default.

Jordan Lee: can we just cap it to 1h until we fix? 24h *by minute* *and* grouping by region/model is asking for it.

Dylan Brooks: jumping in. We need an immediate mitigation (today) + proper fix (rollups/caching). What’s quickest? feature-flag disable the group_by toggles?

Chloe Martin: quick mitigations I can ship:
1) hard cap default window to last 1h (still allow 24h via explicit “Load more”)
2) disallow `group_by=region,model` combined for now (pick one)
3) add response cache (in-memory) keyed by `{experiment_id, route_id, window, group_by}` with 60s TTL

Sean Gallagher: do (1) + (2) now. Cache is good but doesn’t fix first-hit thundering herd if a bunch of ppl click the same dashboard after a deploy.

Allison Grant: what about server-side “top-N regions/models + other”? we don’t need all combos.

Logan Wright: longer-term we should not be grouping raw request_facts at all. We already emit rollups by {route, experiment_id, variant_id, region, model_id} at 5m granularity in analytics. Console should query that.

Mei Lin: if we *must* query request_facts in emergency, add index on `(experiment_id, route_id, bucket_ts)` at minimum. But that’s still heavy for percentile calc.

Caleb Johnson: percentile_cont over millions of rows is expensive. Our rollup tables store precomputed p50/p95/p99 already.

Felix Schneider: why isn’t it using rollup? :thinking:

Chloe Martin: I found it. The endpoint checks “rollup staleness” and if lag > 10m it flips to raw to “avoid stale charts”. Our rollup lag monitor is currently reading ~18m in prod (aggregation job behind).

Rafael Mendes: ^ that matches what I saw earlier: the experiment rollup job is running slower after we added dimensions. Might be backlog from yesterday.

Nadia Rahman: yep cost attribution step got heavier after adding experiment tags. We can reduce by moving cost calc into the rollup job once per bucket (not per request query).

Dylan Brooks: so two parallel tracks:
- Console: stop falling back to raw (or make fallback *coarser* / smaller window)
- Analytics: fix rollup lag

Sean Gallagher: I’m strongly against “fallback to raw” in prod if it can melt the DB. Prefer stale data + banner over taking out control plane.

Allison Grant: agree. We can show “Data is delayed (~18m)” and keep dashboard responsive.

Chloe Martin: proposed change: remove raw fallback entirely for prod, behind `route_experiments_results_raw_fallback` flag default OFF. Only allow for internal staging.

Felix Schneider: +1. UX wise we can add a yellow banner “Results delayed” if rollup staleness > threshold.

Logan Wright: for rollup lag: I can check the aggregator workers. Might need to bump concurrency or reduce cardinality expansions (we had that thread last week).

Mei Lin: also query side: ensure results endpoint is hitting the read replica / analytics store if possible, not primary CP DB.

Caleb Johnson: the rollup dataset lives in analytics DB already. Console results API is still wired to CP DB because it was easiest. We should switch to analytics connection for results.

Dylan Brooks: ok, immediate plan:
1) Chloe: hotfix to disable raw fallback + cap default window to 1h + disable combined group_by (ship behind flag, roll out asap)
2) Logan/Caleb/Mei: unstick rollup lag (today) + confirm where rollup lives and best query path
3) Sean/Rafael: keep an eye on DB CPU, set temporary alert threshold + paging until mitigations are out

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: also add a rate limit / circuit breaker on that endpoint. If `db_cpu>80%`, return 429 with “try again” vs continuing to pile on.

Chloe Martin: I can add a simple guard: if request asks for > 6h window with minute granularity, reject. Also clamp bucket size to 5m/1h depending on window.

Felix Schneider: I’ll open a quick UI patch to make 24h not the default + hide region/model combo toggle until backend fix. (I’ll coordinate with Kenji later)

Rafael Mendes: meanwhile I’m muting non-critical alerts to reduce noise, but keeping the sev2 on CPU.

Deploy Bot: deploy started: console-api hotfix branch `disable-results-raw-fallback` -> prod canary

Logan Wright: update: rollup workers are behind due to a bad query plan on `experiment_variant_metrics_5m` upsert. Mei, I’m DMing you the job logs. Might need an index on conflict target.

Mei Lin: got it. The `ON CONFLICT (experiment_id, route_id, variant_id, bucket_ts, region, model_id)` needs a matching unique index; I don’t think it exists in prod.

Caleb Johnson: wow ok that would absolutely explain backlog.

Nadia Rahman: if we’re adding that unique index, please double-check cardinality constraints (region/model normalized) so we don’t explode row count.

Chloe Martin: canary is live. Raw fallback disabled, default window set to 1h. Combined group_by returns 400 with message “Select a single breakdown (region OR model)”.

Sean Gallagher: DB CPU already trending down: 92% -> 63% in last 5m.

Felix Schneider: dashboard is usable again on my end. Results are slightly delayed but at least page loads.

Allison Grant: please add a postmortem-ish note somewhere: “Never query raw request_facts for percentiles from Console.” This will come up again.

Dylan Brooks: +1. After incident: we should (a) move results to analytics DB (b) add caching (c) ensure rollup index exists via migration (d) keep stale-banner behavior.

Deploy Bot: deploy finished: console-api hotfix -> prod 100%

Rafael Mendes: marking incident mitigated. Keeping watch until rollup backlog is cleared.

Logan Wright: rollup index migration queued for tonight’s maintenance window. In the meantime we increased worker concurrency + reduced per-bucket cost calc.

Sean Gallagher: thanks all. If CPU spikes again, we’ll flip the results feature flag off for affected tenants.

title: #eng-infra: 1738793301
preview:
Vanessa Ortiz: seeing something weird in beta pool partitioning. canary stage went live (stage=1%) and queue depth spiked hard + 429s. looks like canary partition is starving capacity.

Vanessa Ortiz: pool: `dedicated-pool/lat-emb-prod` (beta), region `us-east-1`, reservation 8xH100. stages: 1%/10%/50%/100%. canary got 1 replica.

Irene Choi: do we have a graph? is this just bursty traffic or sustained? also did they have streaming on?

Vanessa Ortiz: grafana link (internal): https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/dedicated-rollout-stage?var-pool=lat-emb-prod&from=now-1h

Vanessa Ortiz: tl;dr at 22:11 UTC, stage promoted to canary. within ~2 min:
- canary `queue_depth_p95` jumps from ~0-3 -> 220+
- canary `request_drop_rate` to 2-5%
- overall pool 429s from ~0.1% -> 1.6% (mostly canary routed)

Peter Holtz: that’s consistent w/ tiny partition + sticky routing. if the canary partition has 1 replica and we route 1% of all traffic, it should be fine *unless* we’re pinning more than intended (sessions/streams) or the traffic split isn’t actually 1%.

Mina Haddad: checking routing logs. we do stage selection at request start, but streaming requests stay pinned to the selected partition (expected). if a client does long streams, it can occupy that partition longer.

Mina Haddad: also: we have a “min 1 replica” for canary, but no guardrail around request shape.

Ravi Menon: quick thought: in Dedicated, “1% traffic” is misleading if request cost distribution is heavy-tailed. 1% of *requests* can be >>1% of *tokens*. did this customer have long context?

Vanessa Ortiz: yep. from their TAM notes, they send 16k context spikes when they re-index. and the canary partition was assigned the newest runtime build (more aggressive batching), so per-request latency might be higher at low concurrency.

Logan Wright: do we have stage-tagged `tokens_in` / `tokens_out` yet? or are we looking at request count only.

Caleb Johnson: stage tagging is partially on. i can query `tokens_out_total{stage_id=canary}` but it’s spotty (some pods missing label).

Vikram Singh: data plane note: when we carve a 1-GPU partition, it’s basically a single queue with continuous batching. if it gets one or two pathological long sequences, it can block smaller ones behind it (HOL). at small N, batching efficiency tanks too.

Emily Zhao: can we confirm how the 1 replica was selected? was it “strict partition” or “shared pool with weighted routing”? if strict, then any spillover is 429.

Mina Haddad: strict partition. routing policy picks partition key; no spillover unless fallback triggers (and fallback is disabled during rollout unless abort). so yeah, stage=canary is hard-isolated.

Rafael Mendes: :eyes: so the canary stage isolation is causing self-inflicted outage for that slice. do we have an auto-abort condition based on queue depth / saturation?

Peter Holtz: not yet for queue depth. gates are mostly error rate + p95 latency + GPU util. but with 1 replica, p95 is noisy + you can get weird partial failures.

Vanessa Ortiz: adding logs snippet. from canary pod `dp-dedicated-rt-7f4c`:
```
2025-02-06T22:13:18.991Z WARN scheduler queue_depth=241 inflight=128 max_inflight=128 stage=canary pool=lat-emb-prod
2025-02-06T22:13:20.102Z WARN admission_control drop reason="queue_full" http_status=429 stage=canary
2025-02-06T22:13:20.103Z INFO request attrs="model=llama-3.1-70b" prompt_tokens=15233 max_tokens=2048 stream=true stage=canary
2025-02-06T22:13:21.447Z WARN gpu oom_count_5m=2 stage=canary
```

Irene Choi: so canary got the worst-case requests (either by chance or by some routing stickiness). with 1 replica, it’s dead.

Ravi Menon: do we have any routing stickiness by org/user that could bias traffic? e.g., consistent hashing by customer_id means a small stage might get “hot” customers disproportionately.

Mina Haddad: yep. for Dedicated we hash on `(account_id, route_id, request_group)` to keep cache locality. if canary partition is selected first for a given hash bucket, it can get consistent load until stage changes.

Peter Holtz: that’s the smoking gun. with 1 replica canary, you can accidentally concentrate a large bucket.

Vikram Singh: +1. traffic % split assumes iid requests. consistent hashing breaks that assumption.

Vanessa Ortiz: so proposed guardrail ideas:
1) enforce min canary partition size >= 2 replicas (or >= 20% for small pools)
2) stage selection should be token-weighted or at least “cost-aware” if we’re going to use hashing
3) allow spillover to stable partition when canary is saturated (but then it’s not a strict test)

Mina Haddad: (3) is tricky for rollback semantics + audit. but we could do “soft isolation” w/ explicit policy: canary preferred, stable fallback on saturation. and still measure canary outcomes.

Rafael Mendes: for beta, we need something fast and safe. i’d take (1) + (gate on queue depth / drops) as immediate.

Logan Wright: i can add a gate signal: `queue_depth_p95` and `admission_drop_rate` per stage. but we need staleness handling.

Caleb Johnson: also we should stop promoting into a stage if sample size is tiny. “min_requests” gate would prevent promoting a 1-replica stage with 5 req/min.

Emily Zhao: infra-side: we should codify in partitioning guidelines: for pools < 10 GPUs, don’t do 1% stage at all. default stages should be replica-based not % based.

Irene Choi: aligns with the ADR discussion (replica partitions for small pools). can we change defaults for Dedicated? (1/10/50/100) doesn’t work for 8 GPUs.

Vanessa Ortiz: agree. for 8 GPUs, stages should be something like 2/4/6/8 replicas or 2/4/8.

Mina Haddad: routing can support both, but we need a rule: if pool_size < N, interpret stages as replica counts; or clamp % to minimum replica count.

Peter Holtz: propose algorithm:
- compute desired replicas = ceil(pool_size * stage_percent)
- clamp to min_replicas_per_stage (2 for canary)
- also require remaining stable replicas >= min_headroom (e.g., 2)
- if not possible, reject plan or suggest alternate stages

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. and also: if using consistent hashing, make stage assignment per request not per bucket, or add salt so buckets distribute. but that will hurt cache locality.

Ravi Menon: maybe keep hashing but hash includes stage_id when stage is < X replicas, so it reshuffles buckets as you promote? you lose some cache but avoid hot bucket.

Mina Haddad: i can add a “small partition mode” where we disable deterministic hashing and do pure weighted random for stage selection. probably ok for canary.

Incident Bot: incident created: INC-2025-02-06-219 "Dedicated rollout canary partition saturation" (severity: SEV-2) https://inc.redwood.internal/INC-2025-02-06-219

Rafael Mendes: action for now: abort rollout on that pool? canary is failing.

Vanessa Ortiz: already told oncall to hold at current stage and route back to stable (manual override). customer is ok.

Mina Haddad: i’ll paste the routing diff showing the stage selection behavior (from `routingd`):
```
// pseudo
partition = consistent_hash(account_id, route_id) % num_partitions
if rollout_enabled:
  stage = rollout_stage_for_partition(partition)
```
so yeah, if one bucket maps to canary, it’s sticky.

Peter Holtz: ok. guardrail: for canary stage, don’t map whole hash buckets. do per-request selection.

Logan Wright: also should alert on canary stage saturation separately. right now overall pool dashboards hide it.

Irene Choi: can we get a PR quickly to clamp canary to >=2 replicas + disable sticky hashing on canary?

Mina Haddad: i can do clamp in routing policy config + add `stage_selection_mode=weighted_random` for canary when replicas < 3. will open PR today.

Vanessa Ortiz: i’ll update infra partitioning guideline doc: “no 1-replica canary partitions” + “small pools use replica-based stages”.

Rafael Mendes: and SRE wants runbook note: if queue_depth spikes in canary, abort/rollback immediately; don’t wait for latency gate (it’ll be noisy).

Caleb Johnson: telemetry fix: stage tag mismatch mentioned earlier. i’ll sync w/ Logan; we need `stage_id` consistently in both router + runtime metrics.

Logan Wright: cool. i’ll add a short-term workaround query that matches either label (`stage` or `stage_id`) until we roll out the fix.

Irene Choi: summary so we don’t lose the thread:
- root cause: 1-replica canary + strict isolation + consistent hashing => hot bucket => queue depth + drops
- mitigations (fast): clamp canary min replicas=2; canary stage selection weighted-random; add gate/alerts on queue depth + drop rate; update guidelines for small pools

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: i’ll post follow-ups in the incident and link to this thread.

Mina Haddad: assigning myself routing changes; will link PR once up.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. please tag me on the PR so we can validate with a replay test before re-enabling beta.

title: #architecture: 1738703321
preview:
Talia Benson: Hey folks — need to schedule a design review for the delivery semantics ADR (audit export). This is the one covering at-least-once, idempotency, checkpointing, replay/backfill. Can we get 45m this week? Target attendees: @Ethan Park @Marcus Lin @Kevin Osei @Sean Gallagher @Rafael Mendes @Logan Wright @Jordan Lee. Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/adr-audit-export-delivery-semantics-and-idempotency (draft)

Ethan Park: +1. We should lock this before we finalize the control plane config API behavior. I can do Thu 11-12 PT or Fri 2-3 PT.

Marcus Lin: I’m in Thu 11 PT. Main question from Sec side: are we *explicitly* saying at-least-once and pushing dedup downstream? We need crisp language for customers + SOC2 evidence.

Kevin Osei: Same. Also need to be super clear on retention vs export. “Guaranteed delivery” gets interpreted as “exactly-once” and “no gaps”, which we can’t promise in outages w/ throttling.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE POV: exactly-once is basically a trap here unless we’re willing to build transactional sinks + state per destination at scale. Prefer at-least-once with bounded replay + tooling. Also need safety valves when downstream is hard-down (auto-pause, max lag).

Rafael Mendes: Agree. If we do exactly-once semantics at the exporter level, we’ll be fighting duplicates *and* missing events forever. At-least-once + stable `event_id` + clear guidance that SIEM should dedup is the sane path.

Logan Wright: Observability angle: at-least-once is easier to reason about in metrics. We can expose lag, retry counts, DLQ rates. Exactly-once claims will generate tickets when customers see duplicates in Splunk.

Jordan Lee: Question: how do we define idempotency key? `event_id` is per event (globally unique) — but exporters will batch. Do we ever need a `delivery_id` per destination attempt?

Benji Okafor: Draft ADR says: primary dedup key is `event_id` (uuid v7-ish) emitted at audit event creation time. Exporter retries can send duplicates; downstream should dedup on `event_id`. For S3, we’re writing files (partitioned) so dedup is more “consumer dedup” unless they ingest into something.

Marcus Lin: I’m ok with `event_id` but we must ensure it’s stable + non-PII and exists for *all* events. Also: how do we handle events emitted pre-v2 schema? Are we going to backfill IDs or just say beta is v2 only?

Ethan Park: Beta will require audit schema v2 (ENG-5127). We can do a compatibility mapping but exporter should only export events that have `event_id`. Otherwise we’ll create non-deduppable events.

Kevin Osei: That’s fine but we need to be explicit in beta docs: “only v2 events exported; older events not backfilled.” Also define backfill window clearly.

Sean Gallagher: Another angle: we should avoid “exactly once” anywhere in docs/UI. It’s not even true for Splunk HEC unless we implement an ack + checkpoint at event granularity and handle every edge case.

Jordan Lee: What if we do: exporter persists checkpoint as “max event_time seen” and “last exported event_id”? That still has ordering assumptions.

Benji Okafor: Current plan is checkpoint by monotonically increasing cursor from the audit store (think `event_seq` / `insert_id`), not `event_time`. Time is not safe for cursoring. ADR mentions “cursor is storage-assigned, strictly increasing per tenant/workspace”.

Rafael Mendes: Yes please. Using timestamps for cursoring will cause gaps during clock skew / delayed ingestion.

Logan Wright: For Splunk: HEC returns 200 even if events are partially accepted? (depends). We should clarify what we consider “delivered”. If we treat HTTP 200 as success for the batch, duplicates will be possible on retry but acceptable.

Marcus Lin: That’s the customer confusion point. We need the language: “at-least-once to destination endpoint; customer should deduplicate using `event_id`.” Plus: “export can be paused during prolonged downstream outage; audit log in Redwood remains source of truth.”

Kevin Osei: +1. Also need explicit mention of DLQ and operator-assisted replay: “failed batches may be queued for replay; replay can result in duplicates.”

Connor O'Brien: On rollout mechanics: we also need a decision on what happens if a config changes while exporter is mid-stream. Do we reset checkpoint? pause? This impacts “duplicates vs missing”.

Ethan Park: Draft behavior: config changes do NOT reset checkpoint by default. If filters/destination change, we continue from last cursor. If customer wants backfill, they set `backfill_window` (bounded). That might re-send older events = duplicates. UI must warn.

Sean Gallagher: Please add guardrails: max backfill window in beta (e.g., 7d) + rate limit replays. Otherwise someone will set 90d and nuke the exporter.

Talia Benson: Ok sounds like the meeting needs to cover:
1) Exactly-once vs at-least-once wording (customer-facing)
2) Cursor/checkpoint model (seq vs time)
3) Replay/backfill semantics + bounds
4) Destination-specific ack semantics (Splunk/Datadog/S3)
5) Safety valves (auto-pause / max lag)

Talia Benson: I’ll schedule Thu 11:00–11:45 PT. If you can’t make it, drop comments in ADR.

Google Calendar: Event created: “ADR Review: Audit Export Delivery Semantics + Idempotency” Thu Feb 6, 11:00–11:45am PT. Guests: Talia Benson, Ethan Park, Marcus Lin, Kevin Osei, Sean Gallagher, Rafael Mendes, Logan Wright, Jordan Lee, Benji Okafor, Connor O'Brien. Location: https://zoom.us/j/987654321

Marcus Lin: Thanks. I’ll add comments tonight on “what we can/can’t claim” for compliance.

Kevin Osei: I’ll also propose FAQ language: “at-least-once, may contain duplicates; use `event_id` for dedup.”

Sean Gallagher: I’ll review the failure modes section and ensure we’re not implicitly promising no duplicates.

Logan Wright: I’ll come with metrics/alerts we need to validate the semantics (lag, retry, DLQ depth).

Ethan Park: Great. Also Jordan/Benji can we make sure ADR calls out idempotency expectations per destination? Splunk/Datadog can dedup in pipeline; S3 is just object store.

Jordan Lee: yep, will add a note: S3 delivery is “at-least-once object writes” (possible duplicate objects) and consumer should handle. We should also use deterministic object keying where possible to reduce duplicates.

Benji Okafor: careful: deterministic keys can lead to overwrite = data loss if partial writes. Prefer unique keys + manifest? We can debate in the review.

Rafael Mendes: +1, overwrites are scary. Unique objects + checkpoint in our DB is safer; duplicates are OK.

Talia Benson: Perfect. See you Thu.
text:
Talia Benson: Hey folks — need to schedule a design review for the delivery semantics ADR (audit export). This is the one covering at-least-once, idempotency, checkpointing, replay/backfill. Can we get 45m this week? Target attendees: @Ethan Park @Marcus Lin @Kevin Osei @Sean Gallagher @Rafael Mendes @Logan Wright @Jordan Lee. Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/adr-audit-export-delivery-semantics-and-idempotency (draft)

Ethan Park: +1. We should lock this before we finalize the control plane config API behavior. I can do Thu 11-12 PT or Fri 2-3 PT.

Marcus Lin: I’m in Thu 11 PT. Main question from Sec side: are we *explicitly* saying at-least-once and pushing dedup downstream? We need crisp language for customers + SOC2 evidence.

Kevin Osei: Same. Also need to be super clear on retention vs export. “Guaranteed delivery” gets interpreted as “exactly-once” and “no gaps”, which we can’t promise in outages w/ throttling.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE POV: exactly-once is basically a trap here unless we’re willing to build transactional sinks + state per destination at scale. Prefer at-least-once with bounded replay + tooling. Also need safety valves when downstream is hard-down (auto-pause, max lag).

Rafael Mendes: Agree. If we do exactly-once semantics at the exporter level, we’ll be fighting duplicates *and* missing events forever. At-least-once + stable `event_id` + clear guidance that SIEM should dedup is the sane path.

Logan Wright: Observability angle: at-least-once is easier to reason about in metrics. We can expose lag, retry counts, DLQ rates. Exactly-once claims will generate tickets when customers see duplicates in Splunk.

Jordan Lee: Question: how do we define idempotency key? `event_id` is per event (globally unique) — but exporters will batch. Do we ever need a `delivery_id` per destination attempt?

Benji Okafor: Draft ADR says: primary dedup key is `event_id` (uuid v7-ish) emitted at audit event creation time. Exporter retries can send duplicates; downstream should dedup on `event_id`. For S3, we’re writing files (partitioned) so dedup is more “consumer dedup” unless they ingest into something.

Jordan Lee: What if we do: exporter persists checkpoint as “max event_time seen” and “last exported event_id”? That still has ordering assumptions.

Benji Okafor: Current plan is checkpoint by monotonically increasing cursor from the audit store (think `event_seq` / `insert_id`), not `event_time`. Time is not safe for cursoring. ADR mentions “cursor is storage-assigned, strictly increasing per tenant/workspace”.

Rafael Mendes: Yes please. Using timestamps for cursoring will cause gaps during clock skew / delayed ingestion.

Logan Wright: For Splunk: HEC returns 200 even if events are partially accepted? (depends). We should clarify what we consider “delivered”. If we treat HTTP 200 as success for the batch, duplicates will be possible on retry but acceptable.

Marcus Lin: That’s the customer confusion point. We need the language: “at-least-once to destination endpoint; customer should deduplicate using `event_id`.” Plus: “export can be paused during prolonged downstream outage; audit log in Redwood remains source of truth.”

Kevin Osei: +1. Also need explicit mention of DLQ and operator-assisted replay: “failed batches may be queued for replay; replay can result in duplicates.”

Connor O'Brien: On rollout mechanics: we also need a decision on what happens if a config changes while exporter is mid-stream. Do we reset checkpoint? pause? This impacts “duplicates vs missing”.

Ethan Park: Draft behavior: config changes do NOT reset checkpoint by default. If filters/destination change, we continue from last cursor. If customer wants backfill, they set `backfill_window` (bounded). That might re-send older events = duplicates. UI must warn.

Sean Gallagher: Please add guardrails: max backfill window in beta (e.g., 7d) + rate limit replays. Otherwise someone will set 90d and nuke the exporter.

1) Exactly-once vs at-least-once wording (customer-facing)
2) Cursor/checkpoint model (seq vs time)

4) Destination-specific ack semantics (Splunk/Datadog/S3)
5) Safety valves (auto-pause / max lag)

title: #eng-platform: 1738550025
preview:
Talia Benson: ok team, per go/no-go prep: we need owners assigned for the v1 launch checklist items for unit econ dashboard. dropping the list here — please claim + confirm ETA.

Talia Benson: Checklist items:
1) SLOs + alerts finalized (freshness/completeness + routing)
2) Runbooks linked from alerts (investigation + backfill steps)
3) Backfill plan for 2025Q1 + throttling window
4) Access controls (feature flag + RBAC allowlist + audit)
5) Comms plan (dogfood -> Finance/Product/Eng rollout) + caveats
6) Finance signoff checklist (methodology + reconciliation report)
7) Model/region/tier dimension “known issues” doc for v1

Dylan Brooks: +1. let’s lock owners today. we’re too close to keep this floating.

Sean Gallagher: I can take (1) SLOs + alert routing, but I’ll need Allison/Aisha for the actual alert rules + dashboard panels.

Allison Grant: yep, I’ll own implementing the alert rules + the oncall routing config. Sean can own the targets + escalation path.

Aisha Bello: I can do the Grafana bits + alert tuning thresholds (reduce noise) once we agree SLOs. Also can add links in alert payloads.

Talia Benson: great. marking (1) as Sean (primary), Allison + Aisha (implementation).

Logan Wright: I’ll take (2) runbooks as primary, but want Caleb to be the “hands-on” owner for the queries + validation steps. Nadia for the token metering parity section.

Caleb Johnson: sure. I can own the “what to check first” decision tree + the warehouse validation queries. I’ll also add the common failure modes we’ve seen (missing region mapping, streaming undercount, ingestion lag).

Nadia Rahman: I’ll add the token metering sanity checks + parity notes (streaming vs non-streaming paths, tool calls). Can also add the “when to page runtime” part.

Talia Benson: perfect. (2) Logan primary, Caleb/Nadia contributors.

Mei Lin: For (3) backfill: I can own running + monitoring backfill jobs, and I’ll coordinate with SRE on throttling so we don’t melt the warehouse again.

Cole Summers: I’ll own the cost attribution inputs side + rate-card version pinning for the backfill window. Also can update the reconciliation view to ensure the backfilled hours line up w/ FP&A cutoffs.

Sean Gallagher: please include a “maintenance window” note + max concurrency settings. last time we had warehouse timeouts.

Mei Lin: yep, I’ll propose a run schedule + throttles and get SRE signoff.

Talia Benson: (3) Mei primary, Cole for cost inputs/versioning, SRE consulted.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I can take (4) access controls. I’ll verify feature flag (internal_unit_econ_dashboard) + RBAC policy. Need Marcus for security review + audit log requirements.

Marcus Lin: ok. I’ll review the RBAC rules + ensure we’re not leaking customer-identifiable info in drilldowns (esp top-N tables). Also want to confirm audit logging is on for page access.

Chloe Martin: note: API endpoints already have authz checks, but UI gating needs to match. Kaitlyn, I’ll point you to the middleware used for Console internal tools.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: thx. I’ll also define the initial allowlist groups (Finance, Product, Eng leads) and who can grant access.

Talia Benson: (4) Kaitlyn primary, Marcus security reviewer, Chloe consulted.

Marissa Cole: I’ll take (5) comms plan + caveats (what’s reliable in v1, what’s not). Talia, can you help with sequencing + stakeholder lists?

Talia Benson: yes, I’ll co-own comms sequencing. I’ll draft the rollout steps + deadlines (Eng->Product->Finance) and get Dylan signoff.

Felix Schneider: pls include a short “how to interpret blended vs marginal cost” blurb. that confusion came up in dogfood.

Marissa Cole: yup, will add a “read this first” section + link to metric dictionary.

Talia Benson: (5) Marissa primary, Talia for rollout coordination.

Rishi Malhotra: For (6) finance signoff checklist: I’ll own the methodology signoff items + reconciliation expectations. Need Laura to confirm final caveats + cadence for rate card updates.

Laura Bennett: agreed. I’ll do final approve on the methodology note once reconciliation report matches within the agreed tolerance band. Also want the dashboard to show which rate card version is active.

Cole Summers: rate card version display is already in the aggregation metadata table; we can surface it in the UI footer or info tooltip.

Chloe Martin: UI can show “Rate card: 2025Q4 v3” in the header. Nora was looking at that pattern already.

Talia Benson: great. (6) Rishi primary, Laura approver, Cole/Chloe for surfacing rate card version.

Jada Williams: On (7) “known issues” doc: I can draft a short list of current limitations + mitigations (e.g., unknown region bucket, tier mapping edge cases, quant profile cardinality constraints). Quentin, can you confirm model aliasing caveats?

Quentin Harris: yep. I’ll add the current model alias mapping behavior + guidance on grouping by model vs model_version. Also note any pending alias changes that could shift trend lines.

Nadia Rahman: add a note re: “confidence indicator” when dimension coverage drops (missing tier/region). even if we don’t show it in v1 UI, we should document how to interpret gaps.

Jada Williams: good call. I’ll include “how to spot incomplete coverage” + link to the internal pipeline health dashboard.

Talia Benson: (7) Jada primary, Quentin + Nadia contributors.

Dylan Brooks: this looks good. please post links to the docs/tickets as you create them. also: hard deadline for checklist complete is EOW so we can do go/no-go Monday.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll post SLO doc draft in #eng-sre for review today, then link here.

Allison Grant: I’ll open the PR to add/adjust alert rules once SLO targets are finalized. Will include runbook links in payload.

Caleb Johnson: I’ll start the runbook doc from the previous incident notes and share for review.

Mei Lin: I’ll propose backfill window + throttling settings and get signoff from SRE + warehouse owners.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I’ll verify RBAC + feature flag in staging today, then schedule prod enablement with audit logging checked.

Marissa Cole: I’ll draft comms + caveats and circulate for Dylan/Laura review.

Talia Benson: thanks all. I’ll turn this into a checklist in the epic + track status async. please reply in-thread with links as they’re ready.
