title: #devex: 1738279205
preview:
Alex Martinez: Kicking off the guided onboarding + “first production launch” checklist work. Goal is to reduce TTFS + TTFP and kill the usual 401/403 + streaming confusion + 429 surprise tickets.

Alex Martinez: Can folks drop in here: (1) who’s DRI for what, and (2) quick brainstorm of checklist steps + what *counts* as “first success”.

Talia Benson: I’ll run program mgmt for this (timeline, weekly sync, dependency tracking). I’ll send a calendar invite for a 30min kickoff.

Nikhil Sharma: PM Hosted API side: I’m in for product decisions + definitions on TTFS/TTFP (and exclusions). Also want to align this with PM-751 so the metric is stable.

Marissa Cole: Console PM: I’ll own where this lives in Console + how deep links work. Dylan/Chloe will own implementation pieces.

Dylan Brooks: Console Eng lead here. We can scope UI to a checklist module + step detail drawer + deep links. Need clarity on step IDs + state model soon so we don’t paint ourselves into a corner.

Chloe Martin: Console backend: I’ll coordinate with Ethan/Logan on progress service API shape + auth. Also: we should be explicit about how steps transition (no “regressions” when events arrive out of order).

Monica Patel: SDK lead. We’ll take “prod-safe defaults” guidance + snippet updates across Python/TS/Go + OpenAI compat. Ibrahim can drive streaming/retry defaults proposal.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. Biggest new-user footguns I see: default timeout too low, no retries on 429/5xx, and streaming disconnects treated as success. We should define acceptance criteria per language.

Logan Wright: Telemetry/platform: I’ll own event schema + emission points proposal (likely server-side gateway/control plane). Chris/Mei can help with funnel + TTFS dashboard once definition is locked.

Chris Osei: I’ll partner with Logan on KPI definitions + data QA checks (freshness, cardinality). But we need a crisp TTFS definition to avoid moving targets.

Wei Chen: Docs/quickstarts: I can own the “first successful call” quickstart updates + make sure the checklist doc links map 1:1. Need the step list + copy direction from Jean-Paul/Design.

Elliot Price: Docs IA: I’ll own redirect/IA updates so users don’t bounce between old quickstarts + new checklist. Also want to ensure “production launch” steps have canonical docs pages (quotas/budgets, model pinning, fallback, observability hooks).

Jean-Paul Laurent: Content: I’ll draft step titles + acceptance criteria + concise copy (Console + docs). Please share any “must include” warnings (rate limits, usage dashboard lag, etc.).

Alex Martinez: Awesome. Let’s talk “first success” definition since it drives the first 2-3 checklist steps + the KPI.

Alex Martinez: Proposed: TTFS = time from signup -> first *successful* API response for a generation/embeddings endpoint (2xx). Not counting 401/403/429. Question: does streaming count only when stream is established? or do we require full completion?

Logan Wright: For clean instrumentation, I’d strongly prefer server-side: first request that returns HTTP 200 and includes `x-redwood-request-id` (or equivalent) + passes auth + is not a dry-run. We can also emit a separate event for “stream established” (first SSE chunk) vs “stream completed”.

Ibrahim Khan: +1 for separating. If we gate TTFS on stream *completion* we’ll make metrics noisy (clients disconnect mid-stream). But “stream established” is a meaningful success moment for onboarding.

Monica Patel: Agree. For non-streaming, “success” could be: response returned and parsed (server can’t tell parse success). But at least server-side 2xx + non-empty `usage.total_tokens` is a decent proxy.

Nikhil Sharma: What endpoints count? Chat + completions + embeddings? I’d include embeddings because some customers start there, but we should avoid “/models list” counting as success.

Chris Osei: If we include embeddings, make sure we don’t count “0-vector” or test payload errors. Maybe acceptance criteria: 2xx AND usage tokens > 0 for gen/chat, and embeddings count > 0 for embeddings.

Wei Chen: From docs POV, quickstart uses chat streaming by default. If TTFS excludes streaming establishment it’ll look “broken” vs what users do.

Marissa Cole: In Console checklist, we can have Step 1: “Make your first API call” (counts on first 2xx gen/chat/embeddings). Step 2: “Try streaming” (counts on stream established). Keeps it intuitive.

Dylan Brooks: Works. We’ll need stable step IDs: `first_successful_request`, `first_streaming_response`, etc. And deep links from step details to quickstart + “create API key” page.

Chloe Martin: Also need edge cases: user rotates keys, multiple workspaces, or runs from CI. Progress should be tenant/org scoped, not per user.

Logan Wright: Yup. Recommend scope = tenant. Event payload should include: tenant_id, user_id (optional), api_key_id (hashed?), sdk_lang, sdk_version, endpoint, response_code, model (bounded), is_streaming.

Jean-Paul Laurent: Quick brainstorm of step list (MVP):
1) Create API key
2) Set env var / connect SDK
3) Make first successful request (TTFS)
4) Try streaming
5) Configure timeouts + retries
6) View usage + costs dashboard
7) Set quota/budget alert
8) Pin model version
9) Configure fallback policy
10) Add basic observability hooks (request id + tracing)

Elliot Price: +1 list. We should be careful with order: “usage dashboard” has lag sometimes; copy should set expectation (“may take up to X min”). Also doc link should go to a single canonical page.

Monica Patel: For step 5, we can ship recommended defaults + a “preset” in SDKs, but checklist acceptance criteria should not require user to change code. Maybe: user has seen recommended config + acknowledged? (or we infer from UA tags / SDK options?)

Logan Wright: We can emit an event when SDK uses “prod preset” (client hint via user-agent tag) but avoid making that required for TTFS. Keep it as separate step completion.

Ibrahim Khan: I can add UA tags + a `retryPreset: "standard"` kind of setting in TS/Python. Go might need a helper.

Alex Martinez: Great. Action items:
- Logan + Chris: propose TTFS/TTFP definition + event names by EOW (tie to PM-751).
- Jean-Paul: draft step copy + acceptance criteria v0 (Console-friendly).
- Wei + Elliot: map steps -> docs/quickstarts; call out gaps.
- Monica/Ibrahim: outline SDK “prod defaults” approach + what we can detect (UA tags, config flags).

Talia Benson: I’ll capture the above + start a shared doc + weekly 30 min sync. Also can we list DRIs explicitly?

Alex Martinez: DRIs:
- Program: Talia
- Product: Nikhil (Hosted), Marissa (Console)
- Console Eng: Dylan/Chloe
- Telemetry: Logan (+ Chris for reporting)
- SDKs: Monica (+ Ibrahim)
- Docs: Elliot (+ Wei)
- Content: Jean-Paul

Marissa Cole: One more q: “signup” timestamp for TTFS start. Is it first user creation? first org created? first API key created?

Chris Osei: We should define start as `tenant_created_at` (org/workspace creation) to be consistent, but also track “time to API key created” as a separate step/funnel.

Logan Wright: Agree. `tenant_created` -> `api_key_created` -> `first_successful_request` funnel.

Nikhil Sharma: Please also exclude internal/dogfood tenants from the main KPI dashboards or tag them. Otherwise rollout will look artificially good/bad.

Dylan Brooks: +1. Feature flag cohorts will also matter for interpretation.

Alex Martinez: Cool. Keep this thread for loose brainstorms; Talia will send the kickoff + doc link shortly. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-platform: 1738200021
preview:
Chloe Martin: we need to settle caching for the unit econ query APIs. perf tests w/ the drilldowns are still spiky, and if Finance is gonna hit QTD views every morning we’re gonna melt the warehouse.

Felix Schneider: +1. for the `GET /internal/unit-econ/timeseries` endpoint we can add a read-through cache keyed on (time_range, filters, group_by). question is TTL. I was thinking 5-10m default.

Mei Lin: FYI the heaviest query is the top-N breakdown by model+region+tier when the user has “include model version” toggled. even w/ the new MV, it’s still ~3-6s cold, and can go 12s when the warehouse is under load.

Logan Wright: Finance wants “fresh” meaning: "if it's 10am, I want to see 9am". but our hourly job doesn’t close until ~15 past the hour on a good day, and we have occasional late-arriving token rows (streaming path).

Rishi Malhotra: jumping in — from FP&A side: for day-to-day monitoring, 1h lag is fine as long as it’s clearly labeled. for quarter-to-date reporting, the bigger issue is consistency/reproducibility. if I refresh the same date range 3 times I don’t want it to move because some late events arrived.

Laura Bennett: +1 to Rishi. also: please do not cache something that looks “official” if it’s actually partial data. we’ve been burned by dashboards where the line looks smooth but it’s missing a tier/region.

Felix Schneider: so if we cache for performance, we need to be explicit in UI. but backend-wise, caching is the only way we can keep p95 under control.

Sean Gallagher: SRE view: we can’t have Finance running ad hoc queries that bypass caches and then complain about timeouts. also we should have different protection levels for interactive use vs “official reporting”. rate limits + caching is fine, but needs to align w/ expectations.

Allison Grant: observability: we can add a response header like `x-data-as-of` and `x-coverage` (ex: % of expected regions/tiers present). then UI can show a confidence indicator.

Chloe Martin: ok but the argument in the other thread was “Finance wants no caching so they see latest”. how do we square it?

Jada Williams: I don’t think it’s “no caching” — it’s “don’t mix partial/late data into a report that gets screenshot into a deck”. we can solve with separate views backed by different tables.

Mei Lin: we already have two levels in the warehouse plan:
- near-real-time aggregates (incremental, tolerant of late rows)
- finance-locked hourly rollups (only includes hours marked COMPLETE)
we could expose both.

Logan Wright: yes, this matches pipeline reality. “live” can be incremental and may revise. “finance” should only use the completed-hour table. late arrivals get picked up next hour/day as an adjustment, not retroactively rewriting closed hours.

Rishi Malhotra: that’s exactly what we want. label it “Finance view (closed hours only)” and include the last closed hour timestamp.

Felix Schneider: concretely: add query param `view=live|finance` (default live for Console). finance uses a different underlying view + different caching semantics.

Chloe Martin: what TTLs though?

Felix Schneider: proposal:
- `view=live`: cache TTL 5m; allow stale-while-revalidate (serve up to 15m stale if warehouse is slow).
- `view=finance`: cache TTL 60m (or align to job cadence) BUT keyed by (rate_card_version, allocation_model_version, time_range, filters). also only returns up to last closed hour, so “freshness” is explicit.

Laura Bennett: I’m ok with 60m TTL if the “finance” endpoint never includes partial current hour and the UI makes it obvious. plus we need an audit trail: rate card version + attribution model version in the response.

Caleb Johnson: from data quality side: finance view is easier to put SLOs on. completeness checks can assert that for each closed hour, expected dims exist (regions, tiers). live view will be noisy.

Sean Gallagher: +1. also: separate alerting. if finance view lags >2h, page someone. if live view is behind, warn but don’t page.

Allison Grant: we can tag alerts by `view`. also add dashboard panels that show cache hit ratio on the API layer so we know if this is actually helping.

Chloe Martin: any objections to having two endpoints instead of a param? e.g. `/unit-econ/live/*` and `/unit-econ/finance/*`

Jada Williams: param is fine as long as authz is enforced. finance view should be restricted to the Finance allowlist + execs (or at least internal RBAC role).

Felix Schneider: param is simpler for clients; we can still do server-side routing. also caching layer key will include the view.

Mei Lin: for finance view, I’d prefer we materialize it with a stable schema & partitions. if it’s just a where-clause on the same live table, we’ll still get unpredictable scan cost.

Logan Wright: agreed. we already have the `unit_econ_hourly_closed` table in the plan for ENG-8426; we’ll make sure finance view hits that.

Rishi Malhotra: can we pin the finance view to “allocation_model_version=v1” for Q1? we can introduce v2 later but we need stable reporting.

Chloe Martin: yes, we’ll include `allocation_model_version` in the response and default finance to v1 unless explicitly requested.

Felix Schneider: action items:
1) I’ll update the API contract sketch to include `view` and required response metadata (`data_as_of`, `rate_card_version`, `allocation_model_version`).
2) Mei to confirm which warehouse view/table powers finance vs live.
3) SRE/Obs to set different caching + alerting policy per view.

Sean Gallagher: pls also add rate limits. live: generous for interactive (burst ok). finance: tighter but predictable. we’ve seen people refresh 20x in a row during exec prep.

Laura Bennett: and in UI, default Finance folks to finance view (closed hours) please. I don’t want screenshots of the live view ending up in the KPI pack.

Chloe Martin: noted. we can set a default based on RBAC role.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: I’ll draft the “data as of” + confidence indicator spec for the UI side.

Felix Schneider: cool. I’ll start a PR for caching split + header metadata (this will likely end up in the PR-19426 bucket).

Chloe Martin: thanks all — decision: ship separate "live" vs "finance" views, different backing tables + different TTLs, clear labeling + metadata. locking this in unless strong objections.

Sean Gallagher: :white_check_mark: works for SRE.

Rishi Malhotra: :white_check_mark: works for FP&A.

Laura Bennett: :white_check_mark: works for Finance, with the labeling + versioning caveats.
text:
Chloe Martin: we need to settle caching for the unit econ query APIs. perf tests w/ the drilldowns are still spiky, and if Finance is gonna hit QTD views every morning we’re gonna melt the warehouse.

Felix Schneider: +1. for the `GET /internal/unit-econ/timeseries` endpoint we can add a read-through cache keyed on (time_range, filters, group_by). question is TTL. I was thinking 5-10m default.

Mei Lin: FYI the heaviest query is the top-N breakdown by model+region+tier when the user has “include model version” toggled. even w/ the new MV, it’s still ~3-6s cold, and can go 12s when the warehouse is under load.

Logan Wright: Finance wants “fresh” meaning: "if it's 10am, I want to see 9am". but our hourly job doesn’t close until ~15 past the hour on a good day, and we have occasional late-arriving token rows (streaming path).

Rishi Malhotra: jumping in — from FP&A side: for day-to-day monitoring, 1h lag is fine as long as it’s clearly labeled. for quarter-to-date reporting, the bigger issue is consistency/reproducibility. if I refresh the same date range 3 times I don’t want it to move because some late events arrived.

Laura Bennett: +1 to Rishi. also: please do not cache something that looks “official” if it’s actually partial data. we’ve been burned by dashboards where the line looks smooth but it’s missing a tier/region.

Felix Schneider: so if we cache for performance, we need to be explicit in UI. but backend-wise, caching is the only way we can keep p95 under control.

Sean Gallagher: SRE view: we can’t have Finance running ad hoc queries that bypass caches and then complain about timeouts. also we should have different protection levels for interactive use vs “official reporting”. rate limits + caching is fine, but needs to align w/ expectations.

Allison Grant: observability: we can add a response header like `x-data-as-of` and `x-coverage` (ex: % of expected regions/tiers present). then UI can show a confidence indicator.

Chloe Martin: ok but the argument in the other thread was “Finance wants no caching so they see latest”. how do we square it?

Jada Williams: I don’t think it’s “no caching” — it’s “don’t mix partial/late data into a report that gets screenshot into a deck”. we can solve with separate views backed by different tables.

title: #eng-platform: 1738186033
preview:
Jada Williams: Need to lock default guardrail thresholds for route experiments (latency/error/cost) before we freeze Console copy. What are we comfortable shipping as defaults for beta + GA?

Paula Mendes: +1. We should pick defaults that are safe (catch real regressions) but not so tight they trigger constantly, esp on low traffic routes.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE POV, defaults must be conservative + predictable. Also we need to be explicit: is this soft-stop only by default? (banner + notify), and hard-stop is opt-in / behind kill switch.

Jada Williams: Yes. Per the earlier thread, default behavior = soft-stop + notification. Hard-stop available but opt-in.

Allison Grant: For latency, I’d suggest p99 + p95. p50 is too noisy. Also recommend comparing variant vs control within same window (not historical baseline) so we don’t conflate daily traffic mix changes.

Logan Wright: Telemetry can support p50/p95/p99 per {route, experiment_id, variant_id, region}. But for guardrails, we should keep it to p95/p99 to reduce confusion.

Nadia Rahman: Cost guardrail definition: cost per 1K output tokens? Or cost per request? Token mix changes can make cost/request misleading.

Paula Mendes: Product wants “Cost / 1K tokens” in the UI (and “$ / request” as secondary). Guardrail should probably use cost per token (or per 1K tokens) so it’s stable across prompt size.

Jordan Lee: Note for routing: if we soft-stop, we set experiment state=degraded but routing stays the same unless operator stops. If we hard-stop, evaluator should remove experiment from consideration (fall back to baseline policy). Need thresholds that won’t flap.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. Also we need minimum sample size gating for guardrails; otherwise a handful of errors can trip error-rate threshold on tiny volume.

Rafael Mendes: Draft proposal (based on what we’ve used internally for canaries):
- Latency guardrail: trigger if variant p99 is > (control p99 * 1.25) OR +250ms absolute (whichever is larger) over a 10m window.
- Secondary latency: p95 > control p95 * 1.20 OR +150ms absolute.
- Error guardrail: trigger if 5xx rate (or “server_error”) is > control by +0.5% absolute AND >1.5x relative over 10m.
- Cost guardrail: cost per 1K tokens > control by +15% over 30m (longer window).
- Requires min N: at least 200 requests per variant in window, and at least 20 errors total for error guardrail.
Thoughts?

Marissa Cole: For Console, we need something we can explain in 1-2 sentences. The “whichever larger” logic is ok but might be confusing. Could we simplify to relative deltas only?

Sean Gallagher: Pure relative deltas are dangerous when control is already very low (e.g., p99 80ms). +25% becomes +20ms which is noise. Absolute floor helps.

Dylan Brooks: UX-wise, we can show “Trigger when p99 is 25% slower (min +250ms)” in tooltip. That’s manageable.

Allison Grant: +1 for absolute floors. Also please include attribution drop rate guardrail? If variant attribution is missing we’ll get weird numbers + angry users.

Logan Wright: That’s more of an experiment health/system guardrail than variant regression. But we can include a separate “data quality” banner: if attribution drop > X% we mark results as unreliable.

Paula Mendes: Let’s keep defaults for experiment guardrails focused on: latency / error / cost. Separate health checks for “config propagation lag” + “attribution drop rate” live in health section.

Felix Schneider: On customization: do we allow per-route overrides for defaults? (e.g., routes with long-running tool calls will have huge p99). I’m worried about a single global default being wrong for some customers.

Jada Williams: Plan was: start with defaults, allow override per experiment (advanced section) + allow per route template (later). But we can ship per-route override in GA if it’s not too much backend work.

Jordan Lee: Backend can support precedence: per-experiment guardrail config overrides route defaults; route defaults override global defaults. But we need to store route defaults in the same policy config model; otherwise it’s messy.

Rafael Mendes: If we don’t have per-route defaults in v1, at least allow per-experiment override + save preset templates (“latency sensitive”, “cost sensitive”).

Nadia Rahman: For cost guardrail window: 30m is safer. Cost signals are spiky (batching/caching) and can be skewed by a few huge prompts. Also define which cost: GPU + platform? Or total billed? We should use the same cost metric Console shows.

Logan Wright: We have “estimated_unit_cost_usd_per_1k_tokens” from the cost attribution pipeline. That’s consistent + available near real-time. Billing numbers lag.

Marissa Cole: Ok, summarizing what I think we’re aligned on:
1) Default guardrail behavior = soft-stop + notifications.
2) Default thresholds:
- p99 latency: 25% regression vs control with absolute floor +250ms (10m window, min 200 req/variant)
- p95 latency: 20% regression vs control with absolute floor +150ms (10m window, min 200 req/variant)
- 5xx error rate: +0.5% absolute AND 1.5x relative vs control (10m window, min 200 req/variant; require >=20 errors to trip)
- Cost per 1K tokens: +15% vs control (30m window, min 200 req/variant)
3) Results reliability banners (not “stop” guardrails): attribution drop rate + propagation lag.
4) Customization: allow per-experiment override in v1; route-level defaults optional (defer if too big).
Can someone confirm / adjust before we update PRD + Console strings?

Sean Gallagher: Confirmed from SRE. Please add: hard-stop remains kill-switchable + opt-in, and guardrail evaluation should require 2 consecutive windows to trigger (reduce flapping).

Paula Mendes: +1 on 2 consecutive windows for defaults. That will read as “sustained regression.”

Jada Williams: Works. I’ll update the guardrail evaluator default to require 2 windows (i.e., 20m for p95/p99/error; 60m for cost) unless operator config changes it.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll sync with Felix on UI copy/tooltips and make sure advanced override doesn’t overwhelm the wizard.

Allison Grant: Please make sure we log the computed deltas + sample sizes into the audit/event stream when a guardrail triggers. Makes oncall triage way faster.

Rafael Mendes: Noted. I’ll add to runbook + ensure alert payload includes: metric, threshold, window, req count, control value, variant value, delta.

Marissa Cole: Great. I’ll post the decision in #product too + update beta enablement doc. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-platform: 1738183029
preview:
Logan Wright: need to settle export flow for usage insights before we finalize API contract. async job vs sync download? and hard limits.
Chloe Martin: +1. console wants “Export CSV” button. UX spec currently assumes async (toast + background job) but FE would love a simple sync `GET` if feasible.
Dylan Brooks: from console perspective: sync is nicer for small ranges, but anything over a couple seconds will feel broken. also we need “your export is ready” state.
Nadia Rahman: re: data size: route+model breakdown can blow up if customers filter poorly (30d + all projects + all routes). we should assume some will do worst-case.
Felix Schneider: what’s our current p95 for the breakdown query at 7d? (interactive)
Logan Wright: warehouse says ~900ms-1.5s for typical 24h/7d, but 30d can go 10-30s depending on cardinality + partitions. exports can be bigger + more columns.
Allison Grant: also keep in mind late events. export should include “data as of” timestamp so we don’t get support tickets comparing to billing on a moving target.
Chloe Martin: ok, so async export seems safer. but do we allow a “small export” fast path?
Marcus Lin: security preference: one consistent server-side export pipeline with audit logging. sync download tends to bypass some of the “export requested” / “export downloaded” event semantics unless we’re careful.
Kevin Osei: yeah, +1. also we need signed URLs with short TTL and log *both* the request and the download. if we do sync streaming directly from API, we still need to treat it as an export and log it.
Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC: export should use same permission gate as cost views + ideally an additional “can_export” check (workspace setting). we have customers who allow viewing but block downloads.
Ethan Park: we can implement: `permission=cost.read` for viewing + `permission=cost.export` for export initiation. workspace setting `costExportsEnabled=false` returns 403 w/ reason.
Dylan Brooks: for FE, async job with polling endpoint is fine. but please don’t make us poll forever; give ETA or at least “queued/running/ready/failed”.
Logan Wright: proposal:
- `POST /v1/usage_insights/exports` -> returns `export_id`
- `GET /v1/usage_insights/exports/{id}` -> status + metadata + `download_url` when ready
- signed URL points to object storage, TTL 15m
- server-side job writes CSV
Chloe Martin: what about synchronous option? like `GET /v1/usage_insights/breakdown.csv` for <= 24h?
Marcus Lin: I’d rather not. too easy to accidentally create a data exfil endpoint that’s not covered by export-specific auditing. if we really need it, it should still create an export record behind the scenes.
Kevin Osei: +1. plus we’ll want throttles/limits around exports (per-user and per-workspace). async makes it easier.
Nadia Rahman: size limits: we should decide row cap and/or bytes cap.
Logan Wright: thinking:
- hard cap: 250k rows or 100MB uncompressed (whichever first)
- time range cap for export: 31 days max (configurable later)
- if cap exceeded, fail with “too_large” and guidance: tighten filters or export at coarser grain.
Felix Schneider: can we estimate row count before running full export? maybe do a preflight `COUNT` on same filters.
Logan Wright: yes. export job can do a lightweight preflight count and bail early.
Allison Grant: please ensure we surface the reason in UI. “Export failed” isn’t enough; include “too many rows” and which dimension is causing explosion (route dimension likely).
Dylan Brooks: +1. give us a structured error:
`{"code":"too_large","message":"Export exceeds 250k rows","hints":["Narrow time range","Filter to a single project","Group by route only"]}`
Chloe Martin: audit logging fields needed? I saw INT ticket about it.
Kevin Osei: minimum:
- event: `usage_insights.export_requested`
- actor_user_id, actor_email (if available), actor_role
- workspace_id, project_id (if filter includes), environment
- filters (NOT raw query text; store normalized filter set + hash)
- requested_at, completed_at
- row_count, byte_count
- export_format (csv)
- include_cost=true/false
and for download:
- event: `usage_insights.export_downloaded`
- export_id, actor_user_id
- source_ip, user_agent
- download_method (console/api)
Marcus Lin: also need “shareable link” later, but for now download URLs should be single-use-ish (short TTL). don’t allow a long-lived link that gets forwarded.
Ethan Park: we can mint signed URL per `GET export/{id}` call. store nothing long-lived except the object key + expiration.
Kaitlyn Nguyen: also: don’t leak whether an export exists to someone without permission. `GET exports/{id}` should 404 (or 403?) consistently.
Marcus Lin: prefer 404 to avoid enumeration.
Chloe Martin: question: if someone initiates export then loses permission (role change) can they still download?
Kaitlyn Nguyen: no. permission check on both initiate and download URL issuance.
Kevin Osei: +1. audit `export_downloaded` only if URL issued, not just status checked.
Cole Summers: jumping in w/ billing correctness angle: exports must match billing totals for the same time window & currency rounding. please include `currency` and `cost_basis` metadata (e.g. “metered_usd” vs “invoiced_usd”).
Logan Wright: we’ll tag export as `cost_basis=metered_estimate` for beta. console copy should say “may differ from invoice due to timing/rounding”.
Dylan Brooks: ok. we’ll add footnote in UI + link to help doc.
Allison Grant: data freshness: export should include `data_freshness_ts` and `aggregation_lag_seconds` in the export metadata (not necessarily in CSV rows).
Chloe Martin: where do we store export metadata? control plane DB?
Ethan Park: simplest: console backend DB table `usage_insights_exports` w/ `export_id`, `workspace_id`, `created_by`, `status`, `object_key`, `row_count`, `byte_count`, `filters_json`, `filters_hash`, timestamps. object stored in our standard exports bucket.
Kevin Osei: retention: export objects should expire quickly (24h?) unless compliance requires shorter. and we should respect restricted workspaces (no export at all).
Marcus Lin: default expiry 24h is fine for beta. restricted workspaces: disable and show “Exports disabled by admin”.
Nadia Rahman: performance: exports should run on a separate queue / worker pool, not the interactive query service.
Logan Wright: agreed. analytics worker queue with concurrency limits per workspace to avoid warehouse pileups.
Felix Schneider: any rate limits on export creation?
Logan Wright: propose: 5 exports / user / hour, 20 exports / workspace / hour for beta. also deny concurrent exports with identical filters (dedupe on filters_hash for 10m).
Dylan Brooks: dedupe is nice. UI can show “already running”.
Chloe Martin: ok sounds like consensus: async-only export job w/ size caps, short-lived signed URLs, audit requested+downloaded, RBAC on both.
Marcus Lin: :thumbsup: please capture in API contract + security checklist.
Kevin Osei: :thumbsup: I’ll paste a sample audit payload later today.
DeployBot: (automated) Reminder: security review checklist for new data export endpoints is in go/security/export-checklist v3.
Logan Wright: action items:
1) Logan: update API contract draft w/ export job lifecycle + limits
2) Chloe/Dylan: confirm FE states + error copy for too_large / permission_denied
3) Kevin/Marcus/Kaitlyn: finalize audit fields + 404 vs 403 guidance
4) Nadia/Allison: add freshness + cost_basis metadata requirements
Cole Summers: add me to the contract review when it’s up—need to confirm rounding rules in CSV.
Chloe Martin: will do. thanks all.
text:
Logan Wright: need to settle export flow for usage insights before we finalize API contract. async job vs sync download? and hard limits.
Chloe Martin: +1. console wants “Export CSV” button. UX spec currently assumes async (toast + background job) but FE would love a simple sync `GET` if feasible.
Dylan Brooks: from console perspective: sync is nicer for small ranges, but anything over a couple seconds will feel broken. also we need “your export is ready” state.
Nadia Rahman: re: data size: route+model breakdown can blow up if customers filter poorly (30d + all projects + all routes). we should assume some will do worst-case.
Felix Schneider: what’s our current p95 for the breakdown query at 7d? (interactive)
Logan Wright: warehouse says ~900ms-1.5s for typical 24h/7d, but 30d can go 10-30s depending on cardinality + partitions. exports can be bigger + more columns.
Allison Grant: also keep in mind late events. export should include “data as of” timestamp so we don’t get support tickets comparing to billing on a moving target.
Chloe Martin: ok, so async export seems safer. but do we allow a “small export” fast path?
Marcus Lin: security preference: one consistent server-side export pipeline with audit logging. sync download tends to bypass some of the “export requested” / “export downloaded” event semantics unless we’re careful.
Kevin Osei: yeah, +1. also we need signed URLs with short TTL and log *both* the request and the download. if we do sync streaming directly from API, we still need to treat it as an export and log it.
Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC: export should use same permission gate as cost views + ideally an additional “can_export” check (workspace setting). we have customers who allow viewing but block downloads.
Ethan Park: we can implement: `permission=cost.read` for viewing + `permission=cost.export` for export initiation. workspace setting `costExportsEnabled=false` returns 403 w/ reason.
Dylan Brooks: for FE, async job with polling endpoint is fine. but please don’t make us poll forever; give ETA or at least “queued/running/ready/failed”.
Logan Wright: proposal:
- `POST /v1/usage_insights/exports` -> returns `export_id`
- `GET /v1/usage_insights/exports/{id}` -> status + metadata + `download_url` when ready
- signed URL points to object storage, TTL 15m
- server-side job writes CSV
Chloe Martin: what about synchronous option? like `GET /v1/usage_insights/breakdown.csv` for <= 24h?
Marcus Lin: I’d rather not. too easy to accidentally create a data exfil endpoint that’s not covered by export-specific auditing. if we really need it, it should still create an export record behind the scenes.
Kevin Osei: +1. plus we’ll want throttles/limits around exports (per-user and per-workspace). async makes it easier.
Nadia Rahman: size limits: we should decide row cap and/or bytes cap.
Logan Wright: thinking:
- hard cap: 250k rows or 100MB uncompressed (whichever first)
- time range cap for export: 31 days max (configurable later)
- if cap exceeded, fail with “too_large” and guidance: tighten filters or export at coarser grain.
Felix Schneider: can we estimate row count before running full export? maybe do a preflight `COUNT` on same filters.
Logan Wright: yes. export job can do a lightweight preflight count and bail early.
Allison Grant: please ensure we surface the reason in UI. “Export failed” isn’t enough; include “too many rows” and which dimension is causing explosion (route dimension likely).
Dylan Brooks: +1. give us a structured error:
`{"code":"too_large","message":"Export exceeds 250k rows","hints":["Narrow time range","Filter to a single project","Group by route only"]}`
Chloe Martin: audit logging fields needed? I saw INT ticket about it.
Kevin Osei: minimum:
- event: `usage_insights.export_requested`
- actor_user_id, actor_email (if available), actor_role
- workspace_id, project_id (if filter includes), environment
- filters (NOT raw query text; store normalized filter set + hash)
- requested_at, completed_at
- row_count, byte_count
- export_format (csv)
- include_cost=true/false
and for download:
- event: `usage_insights.export_downloaded`
- export_id, actor_user_id
- source_ip, user_agent
- download_method (console/api)
Marcus Lin: also need “shareable link” later, but for now download URLs should be single-use-ish (short TTL). don’t allow a long-lived link that gets forwarded.
Ethan Park: we can mint signed URL per `GET export/{id}` call. store nothing long-lived except the object key + expiration.
Kaitlyn Nguyen: also: don’t leak whether an export exists to someone without permission. `GET exports/{id}` should 404 (or 403?) consistently.
Marcus Lin: prefer 404 to avoid enumeration.
Chloe Martin: question: if someone initiates export then loses permission (role change) can they still download?
Kaitlyn Nguyen: no. permission check on both initiate and download URL issuance.
Kevin Osei: +1. audit `export_downloaded` only if URL issued, not just status checked.
Cole Summers: jumping in w/ billing correctness angle: exports must match billing totals for the same time window & currency rounding. please include `currency` and `cost_basis` metadata (e.g. “metered_usd” vs “invoiced_usd”).
Logan Wright: we’ll tag export as `cost_basis=metered_estimate` for beta. console copy should say “may differ from invoice due to timing/rounding”.
Dylan Brooks: ok. we’ll add footnote in UI + link to help doc.
Allison Grant: data freshness: export should include `data_freshness_ts` and `aggregation_lag_seconds` in the export metadata (not necessarily in CSV rows).
Chloe Martin: where do we store export metadata? control plane DB?
Ethan Park: simplest: console backend DB table `usage_insights_exports` w/ `export_id`, `workspace_id`, `created_by`, `status`, `object_key`, `row_count`, `byte_count`, `filters_json`, `filters_hash`, timestamps. object stored in our standard exports bucket.
Kevin Osei: retention: export objects should expire quickly (24h?) unless compliance requires shorter. and we should respect restricted workspaces (no export at all).
Marcus Lin: default expiry 24h is fine for beta. restricted workspaces: disable and show “Exports disabled by admin”.
Nadia Rahman: performance: exports should run on a separate queue / worker pool, not the interactive query service.
Logan Wright: agreed. analytics worker queue with concurrency limits per workspace to avoid warehouse pileups.
Felix Schneider: any rate limits on export creation?
Logan Wright: propose: 5 exports / user / hour, 20 exports / workspace / hour for beta. also deny concurrent exports with identical filters (dedupe on filters_hash for 10m).
Dylan Brooks: dedupe is nice. UI can show “already running”.
Chloe Martin: ok sounds like consensus: async-only export job w/ size caps, short-lived signed URLs, audit requested+downloaded, RBAC on both.
Marcus Lin: :thumbsup: please capture in API contract + security checklist.
Kevin Osei: :thumbsup: I’ll paste a sample audit payload later today.
DeployBot: (automated) Reminder: security review checklist for new data export endpoints is in go/security/export-checklist v3.
Logan Wright: action items:
1) Logan: update API contract draft w/ export job lifecycle + limits
2) Chloe/Dylan: confirm FE states + error copy for too_large / permission_denied
3) Kevin/Marcus/Kaitlyn: finalize audit fields + 404 vs 403 guidance
4) Nadia/Allison: add freshness + cost_basis metadata requirements
Cole Summers: add me to the contract review when it’s up—need to confirm rounding rules in CSV.
Chloe Martin: will do. thanks all.

title: #eng-platform: 1738107655
preview:
Mei Lin: seeing a mismatch in the reconciliation view vs billing totals for last week. dashboard shows ~6.3% lower $ than billing for hosted/self-serve in us-east. anyone else looking at this?

Cole Summers: is this from the new reconciliation report view (ENG-8430) or ad-hoc?

Mei Lin: from `finance.unit_econ_recon_daily_v1` (the one from pr-19390). comparing to billing export totals by day.

Rishi Malhotra: yup, i flagged the same in the KPI pack draft. for Jan 25, billing says $128,442 (hosted), dashboard alloc says $120,081. tokens are closer though.

Laura Bennett: we need to understand if it’s a definition gap vs a bug. reminder: finance signoff assumes we can reconcile within ~1-2% at monthly granularity.

Logan Wright: can you drop the exact filters / query? this smells like (a) sampling window mismatch (UTC vs PT day boundary) or (b) tier mapping excluding some routes.

Mei Lin: query:
```
select
  usage_date,
  sum(allocated_cost_usd) as dash_cost,
  sum(output_tokens) as dash_out_tokens
from analytics.unit_econ_hourly_v1
where usage_date between '2025-01-20' and '2025-01-26'
  and customer_tier = 'self_serve'
  and region = 'us-east'
group by 1
order by 1;
```
then i compare to billing daily totals for the same date range + region tag.

Chloe Martin: quick q: billing “region tag” is based on customer-selected region or actual execution region? we have both in gateway logs.

Cole Summers: billing export uses `execution_region` (from request logs) for hosted. dedicated/private are separate.

Nadia Rahman: possible culprit: streaming path undercount output tokens -> would under-allocate cost if we use output tokens as denominator. but you said tokens are close?

Rishi Malhotra: tokens are within ~1-2% daily. dollars are the bigger gap.

Caleb Johnson: do we include overhead + idle in dashboard but billing is “direct”? or vice versa?

Cole Summers: current attribution model in adr-034: we allocate GPU+shared overhead to tiers based on output tokens, incl idle for hosted pools. billing is what we charged customers, which includes margin + discounts + credits.

Laura Bennett: important: reconciliation report should compare apples-to-apples. it shouldn’t be comparing to “customer billed revenue”. it should compare to “infra cost” inputs.

Mei Lin: right, but the recon view claims to compare “dashboard aggregates vs billing totals” (wording in doc). i assumed it meant billing usage totals (not revenue) pulled from metering/billing pipeline.

Rishi Malhotra: in finance, when we say “billing totals” we typically mean usage charges net of discounts/credits (still revenue-ish). maybe we’re mixing terms.

Dylan Brooks: ^ this is likely the confusion. we need two recon modes: (1) metering parity recon (tokens/requests) vs billing usage pipeline, and (2) cost rollup recon vs finance rate card/GL. if we’re using revenue numbers, dashboard cost won’t match by design.

Mei Lin: ok but the dataset i’m comparing against is “billing usage summary” (not invoices). it’s basically usage * internal rate (no margin), but it does apply plan-level multipliers.

Cole Summers: plan-level multipliers = that’s still not cost. the dashboard uses GPU hourly costs + allocation.

Allison Grant: also note: dashboard cost includes non-billable traffic (internal dogfood, retries, some failed requests depending on inclusion rules). billing usage excludes those.

Aisha Bello: do we have a breakdown of “excluded from billing” categories? would help to explain deltas.

Caleb Johnson: we can quantify via dimensions: `billable_flag`, `request_outcome`, `retry_count`? do we have those in unit_econ_hourly?

Nadia Rahman: not all. we have `is_billable` in gateway metering, but it didn’t make it into the econ aggregation v1 (cardinality concerns). we do have `customer_id` stripped, so hard to join.

Mei Lin: recon view joins econ aggregates to billing usage by (date, model, region, tier). if billing excludes non-billable and econ includes it, you’d expect dashboard to be higher, not lower.

Logan Wright: unless we’re dropping some cost rows due to missing dimensions in econ, which would make dashboard lower.

Caleb Johnson: +1. any “unknown region” being dropped? we had a null-handling bug in region mapping recently.

Mei Lin: checking `region = 'unknown'`:
```
select usage_date, sum(allocated_cost_usd)
from analytics.unit_econ_hourly_v1
where usage_date between '2025-01-20' and '2025-01-26'
  and customer_tier = 'self_serve'
  and region = 'unknown'
group by 1;
```
results are basically 0. which is suspicious because raw metering has some null regions.

Cole Summers: if mapping turns null -> dropped row before aggregation, you’d lose both tokens + cost allocation for those requests.

Nadia Rahman: in metering ingestion, we set region to `''` for a few paths (legacy completions endpoint) until pr-19361. might still be happening for some routes.

Chloe Martin: do we have an easy way to compare raw token totals vs aggregated token totals for same window? like completeness check.

Caleb Johnson: metricsbot already alerting on missing dimension coverage but only at overall level, not by endpoint.

redwood-metricsbot: completeness check (unit_econ_hourly_v1) for 2025-01-25 00:00-23:00 UTC: model_id coverage 99.6%, region coverage 92.1% (threshold 95%) :warning:

Aisha Bello: there we go. region coverage 92% means 8% of tokens missing region -> those rows likely excluded from the view if query filters `region='us-east'` etc.

Mei Lin: yep. if those requests had blank region they wouldn’t show up in `us-east` filter. but why wouldn’t billing have the same gap?

Cole Summers: billing usage pipeline might backfill region using customer config or gateway host header when execution region missing. econ pipeline is stricter (drops unknown to avoid mis-attribution).

Rishi Malhotra: that would explain “billing higher” for a region slice. but the mismatch i saw was overall hosted total too (no region filter).

Logan Wright: is your “overall hosted total” still filtering out unknown? some queries exclude nulls implicitly.

Mei Lin: i used `where region in ('us-east','us-west','eu-west')` to match finance report tabs. didn’t include unknown.

Laura Bennett: so finance report is excluding unknown too? that’s a process issue. we need a single rule: unknown rolls up into “Unattributed/Unknown” bucket, not dropped.

Dylan Brooks: action items:
1) make econ aggregates retain `region='unknown'` rows (don’t drop) and surface them in dashboard w/ warning.
2) recon query must include unknown bucket so totals reconcile.
3) document that regional slices may not sum to global if unknown >0.

Nadia Rahman: + also fix the remaining legacy path that emits blank region. i’ll open a task against gateway metering.

Caleb Johnson: i can add a DQ check specifically: `% unknown region` by route + customer_tier, alert if >1% for >2h.

Aisha Bello: and we should update the SLO doc to include “unknown bucket exists and is <X%” not “coverage %”. easier to interpret.

Mei Lin: for immediate unblock: if i include unknown bucket, overall cost delta shrinks to ~1.4% for Jan 20-26. regional delta still ~6% because unknown is large and billing assigns it to us-east.

Cole Summers: that’s expected given their heuristic. for finance / exec reporting, we should probably keep unknown separate; don’t silently attribute.

Rishi Malhotra: agreed. but finance needs guidance on how to treat unknown in QTD. either exclude consistently from both sides, or include as separate line item.

Laura Bennett: include as separate line item. excluding makes it too easy to hide data quality issues.

Chloe Martin: dashboard UX: if region filter = “All”, include unknown in totals. if region filter = specific region, unknown excluded (obviously). but we should show a callout: “X% of usage has unknown region and is excluded from this view.”

Allison Grant: i’ll sync w/ Alicia (design) on the callout + confidence indicator. this matches the earlier proposal.

Mei Lin: i’ll patch the recon view query + add an “unknown” row for finance export. also will add a note to the confluence metric defs.

Dylan Brooks: please link this thread in ENG-8430 follow-up. we need the recon report wording fixed (billing usage vs revenue vs cost).

Rishi Malhotra: i’ll update the KPI pack draft to include “Unattributed (unknown region)” and annotate that it’s a metering gap, not an ops cost change.

Laura Bennett: thanks. once unknown is visible, we can set an acceptable threshold for signoff.

Caleb Johnson: posting a quick query for anyone validating:
```
-- global hosted self-serve incl unknown
select usage_date,
       sum(allocated_cost_usd) dash_cost,
       sum(output_tokens) dash_out_tokens,
       sum(case when region='unknown' then output_tokens else 0 end) unknown_out_tokens
from analytics.unit_econ_hourly_v1
where usage_date between '2025-01-20' and '2025-01-26'
  and customer_tier='self_serve'
group by 1
order by 1;
```

Mei Lin: :thumbsup: running now. will report back in a few.

Mei Lin: update: unknown_out_tokens is 7.8% on Jan 25. that basically matches the 6-8% delta people were seeing on regional tabs.

Logan Wright: cool, so root cause = missing region dimension on a subset of hosted requests + recon/report excluding unknown bucket. we’ll fix both.

Aisha Bello: can we get an owner/date for the gateway fix? want to avoid this lingering into month-end.

Nadia Rahman: i’ll take it. targeting tomorrow EOD for PR, rollout behind flag. will ping here when merged.

Dylan Brooks: closing the loop: until the fix lands, dashboard should show the confidence indicator for region views, otherwise people will keep asking why sums don’t match.

Allison Grant: ack. i’ll also tune the metricsbot alert message to explicitly mention unknown% and link runbook section.

Laura Bennett: please keep me posted. finance signoff depends on the recon story being clean + documented.

title: #eng-platform: 1738095544
preview:
Kevin Osei: Quick q on `schema_version` strategy for audit events + exporter normalization. For v1 rollout: do we require every producer to set `schema_version`, or can exporter backfill it when it recognizes a legacy shape?

Kevin Osei: Context: exporter is currently seeing a mix of shapes in the sink (some older control plane stuff still has `tenantId`, `action`, etc). Need something deterministic for SIEM customers.

Michael Nguyen: My take: producers MUST emit `schema_version` once they adopt v1. But exporter should also *normalize* legacy events and set `schema_version` on the *normalized* output.

Michael Nguyen: Otherwise customers get a split-brain stream forever.

Nadia Rahman: +1. Telemetry side also needs to segment by version. If `schema_version` is missing, dashboards become “maybe v1, maybe not” :grimacing:

Allison Grant: we should be explicit in the schema doc: `schema_version` is required field for v1 payloads.

Allison Grant: exporter behavior is a separate “compat layer” that outputs v1 even if input is legacy. (And should preserve raw fields somewhere if needed.)

Kevin Osei: Okay, but what do we call it when exporter is the one setting it? Do we need `schema_version` + `source_schema_version` or something?

Michael Nguyen: I’d avoid adding more fields unless we need it. But we can put the raw version (or “legacy”) in an extension namespace.

Michael Nguyen: e.g. `ext.exporter.raw_schema_version` or `ext.exporter.normalized_from`.

Logan Wright: For coverage dashboards: we only care about the version of the event *after* normalization, because that’s what consumers see.

Logan Wright: But it’d be nice to slice “how much legacy is still flowing” for rollout tracking.

Diego Santos: Also consider Private: customers can lag on exporter upgrades. If we ship producers that start emitting v1, older exporter should pass it through without breaking.

Diego Santos: So: v1 events must be “additive / tolerant” on the exporter side. No strict reject unless explicitly configured.

Ethan Park: Agree. Exporter should validate *outgoing* payload against v1 schema but be lenient on input.

Ethan Park: In other words, exporter owns: (1) normalization, (2) tagging `schema_version`, (3) optional drop/alert on un-normalizable events.

Kevin Osei: That matches what I’m implementing in pr-842. Just want to confirm the exact tagging rules.

Kevin Osei: Here’s an example legacy event we still see:

```json
{
  "id": "7b1d9e74-acde-4e6a-9d10-9f9f2f8a9f2b",
  "time": "2025-01-29T21:18:02.901Z",
  "tenantId": "tnt_4h2k...",
  "action": "rbac.role.update",
  "actor": {"email": "admin@customer.com"},
  "resource": {"type": "role", "id": "role_123"},
  "ip": "203.0.113.4"
}
```

Kevin Osei: Proposed normalized output:

```json
{
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "event_id": "7b1d9e74-acde-4e6a-9d10-9f9f2f8a9f2b",
  "event_time": "2025-01-29T21:18:02.901Z",
  "event_type": "rbac.role.updated",
  "tenant_id": "tnt_4h2k...",
  "actor_type": "user",
  "actor_id": "email:admin@customer.com",
  "actor_display_name": "admin@customer.com",
  "source_ip": "203.0.113.4",
  "target_type": "rbac.role",
  "target_id": "role_123",
  "outcome": "success",
  "ext.exporter.normalized_from": "legacy"
}
```

Allison Grant: Looks reasonable. Minor nit: `event_type` should match taxonomy string exactly (we said past-tense verbs, right?). For RBAC updates I think we agreed on `rbac.role.updated` (so your normalized one is good) vs `rbac.role.update`.

Michael Nguyen: +1 and `event_time` should be RFC3339 with timezone (you have `Z`, so ok).

Nadia Rahman: for `actor_id`, prefixing like `email:` is fine as long as it’s consistent and we document it. Alternative is hashing, but then support/debug gets harder.

Allison Grant: we should avoid raw emails where possible, but audit logs often need it. If we keep email as display name, we should classify it (PII) + ensure retention guidance is clear.

Kevin Osei: yep, redaction doc will say: allow email in actor_display_name but don’t log arbitrary request payloads.

Ethan Park: Question: do we ever want `schema_version: "legacy"`?

Michael Nguyen: I’d avoid non-numeric schema versions. Some SIEMs assume sortable semantic-ish versions.

Michael Nguyen: Better: missing => exporter sets `schema_version: "1.0"` (because output is v1), and store `ext.exporter.input_shape: "legacy"`.

Logan Wright: +1. If schema_version means “shape of this event”, then after normalization it’s v1.

Diego Santos: Another corner: what about events that already have `schema_version` but are wrong (e.g., someone ships `"v1"`)?

Kevin Osei: I can implement: if producer provided `schema_version` and it doesn’t match `^\d+\.\d+$`, exporter rewrites to `1.0` + adds `ext.exporter.schema_version_rewritten=true` and increments a metric.

Allison Grant: That’s good. Prefer metrics + alert over hard dropping.

Ethan Park: +1 best-effort; don’t break admin paths.

Marcus Lin: Aligning on decision:
- `schema_version` is required for v1 producers (engineering standard)
- Exporter normalizes legacy -> emits v1 output with `schema_version: "1.0"`
- Exporter preserves provenance in `ext.exporter.*` (input_shape / normalized_from / rewrite flags)
- No `schema_version: "legacy"` in outbound payloads
- Exporter should be tolerant on input, strict(er) on output + emit metrics for anything it has to rewrite / can’t normalize

Marcus Lin: Kevin, can you add a short section to exporter README + link to schema versioning policy doc when it’s ready? :thumbsup:

Kevin Osei: yep will do. I’ll also add a couple unit tests for the `tenantId` -> `tenant_id` and `action` -> `event_type` mapping.

Logan Wright: If you can emit a counter like `audit_exporter_normalization_total{reason="legacy_missing_schema_version"}`, I can wire it into rollout dashboards.

Kevin Osei: done. Will add `reason` labels for: missing_schema_version, invalid_schema_version, legacy_field_map, unknown_event_type.

Allison Grant: last thing: for unknown event types, don’t invent. Map if known, else keep `event_type: "unknown"` + `ext.exporter.raw_action` and page the owner.

Michael Nguyen: +1. Also make sure we preserve `event_id` always. If legacy has no id, exporter should generate one (ULID?) + set `ext.exporter.generated_event_id=true`.

Kevin Osei: ack. We already discussed ULID for ordering; I’ll use ULID and add that flag.

Diego Santos: can we document the “heuristics” explicitly? Customers will ask how we decide something is legacy vs v1.

Kevin Osei: I’ll add a short table:
- if `schema_version` present and parseable => treat as v1-ish
- else if `tenantId` or `action` present => legacy normalization path
- else => unknown, best-effort map + metric

Marcus Lin: Sounds good. Let’s keep it simple and measurable.
text:

Kevin Osei: Context: exporter is currently seeing a mix of shapes in the sink (some older control plane stuff still has `tenantId`, `action`, etc). Need something deterministic for SIEM customers.

Michael Nguyen: My take: producers MUST emit `schema_version` once they adopt v1. But exporter should also *normalize* legacy events and set `schema_version` on the *normalized* output.

Michael Nguyen: Otherwise customers get a split-brain stream forever.

Nadia Rahman: +1. Telemetry side also needs to segment by version. If `schema_version` is missing, dashboards become “maybe v1, maybe not” :grimacing:

Allison Grant: we should be explicit in the schema doc: `schema_version` is required field for v1 payloads.

Allison Grant: exporter behavior is a separate “compat layer” that outputs v1 even if input is legacy. (And should preserve raw fields somewhere if needed.)

title: #eng: 1738016620
preview:
Dylan Brooks: quick perf review on the usage insights breakdown queries. seeing p95 ~6-9s for 7d on a mid-size workspace in staging. 30d is basically unusable (timeouts). we need ideas before we lock the API contract.

Chloe Martin: yeah, current query path is doing: route_normalization view -> join metering_events (raw) -> group by workspace_id, project_id, route, model, day. It’s scanning way too much.

Felix Schneider: pulled a trace from the query service. breakdown endpoint is hitting warehouse with ~3.2TB scanned for 30d (🤢). Also we’re doing `COUNT(DISTINCT request_id)` and joining retries/fallback events from the same raw table.

Logan Wright: not surprising. raw metering_events is partitioned by event_ts but we’re not getting good partition pruning b/c the query wraps timestamp filters in some date_trunc + timezone conversion.

Mei Lin: +1. In the query I saw `WHERE date_trunc('day', convert_timezone('UTC', :tz, event_ts)) BETWEEN ...` which forces compute on every row.

Dylan Brooks: yep. That’s from the API supporting timezone-based day boundaries. We can move TZ boundary logic to the app and pass UTC bounds?

Nadia Rahman: careful: finance wants day buckets aligned w/ workspace timezone for exports. but we can still pass UTC start/end and do the trunc in UTC if we precompute a “workspace_local_day” at ingest/agg time.

Thomas Baird: perf suggestion: don’t query raw for console. we should ship an aggregated fact table keyed by (workspace_id, project_id, hour_bucket/day_bucket, route_template, model_family, model_version_bucket, deployment_mode, region). Then the console query becomes a small scan + simple group/rollup.

Chloe Martin: we’re already implementing the aggregation job (ENG-3812). but current staging is still querying raw because the agg table is missing token breakdown + cache stats.

Mei Lin: we can extend the agg schema to include the expensive stuff so the breakdown query is a single table read:
- tokens_in, tokens_out
- cache_hit_tokens, cache_miss_tokens (or hit_rate numerator/denominator)
- retry_count, fallback_count
- streaming_request_count (or streaming_tokens_out)
- latency_ms_sum + latency_ms_p50/p95? (latency percentiles are tricky)

Allison Grant: for latency, we probably don’t want to compute percentiles ad hoc for every filter. if we need p95 in-console, either:
1) maintain t-digest sketches per key per hour/day (warehouse-specific), or
2) precompute fixed buckets (e.g., <250ms, 250-500, 500-1s, 1-2s, 2-5s, >5s) and derive “latency-correlated spend” from those.

Felix Schneider: right now the query is doing a join against `request_latency_events` anyway. it’s another full scan.

Logan Wright: materialized view option: create MV `usage_insights_route_model_hourly` from metering_events w/ the dims we care about + route normalized at write time. then query service just filters + aggregates to requested time grain.

Thomas Baird: +1, MV or scheduled job. if we go w/ job, do hourly partitions + cluster by workspace_id, project_id, route_template. that should keep scans bounded.

Dylan Brooks: do we have numbers on cardinality? route_template might blow up if normalization misses.

Chloe Martin: in staging data, top offenders are OpenAI-compat endpoints where path params aren’t templated consistently. That’s in ENG-3813. once we canonicalize, route cardinality is manageable.

Nadia Rahman: also model aliases are causing duplicates right now. if we aggregate on raw model_id, we’ll still have weirdness. we should aggregate on (model_family, model_version_bucket) and keep model_id for drilldown only.

Mei Lin: +1. plus, we can keep a separate “model_id -> bucket” mapping table and update it, but aggregation should store the bucketed dims for stable reporting.

Felix Schneider: tactical fix for this week: change the breakdown query to hit the agg table for tokens + cost, and only join raw for the “what changed” explanations if needed.

Allison Grant: i’d rather we don’t have the console path ever touching raw for beta. observability-wise it’s hard to SLO if query shape changes per filter.

Logan Wright: another easy win: restrict time ranges for interactive queries. e.g. hard cap 14d for breakdown view unless user requests export (async). export can run heavier queries w/ longer timeout.

Dylan Brooks: product will push back on caps but we can justify with “interactive perf.” export endpoint can do 30d+.

Chloe Martin: also pagination. we’re currently doing `ORDER BY cost DESC LIMIT 500` but without pre-aggregation it still computes global group-by first. With pre-agg it becomes cheap.

Thomas Baird: indexes / clustering keys matter: if warehouse supports it, cluster on (workspace_id, day_bucket) then secondary on (route_template, model_bucket). most queries are constrained by workspace + time.

Mei Lin: and don’t compute “totals” as a separate query. we can add a rollup row in the agg table or compute it from same dataset (less round trips).

Felix Schneider: i’ll paste the worst offender query plan summary:
```
Scan metering_events (partitioned by event_ts): 29,184 partitions
Filter: computed expression on event_ts prevents pruning
Join route_normalized_view: broadcast
Group by: workspace_id, project_id, route, model
Distinct: request_id
Spill: yes (disk)
Total time: 48.2s (timed out at 60s)
```

Dylan Brooks: ok. action items?

Logan Wright: propose:
1) adjust API to pass UTC bounds, no tz conversion inside WHERE
2) prioritize MV/job for `usage_insights_route_model_hourly` including tokens/cache/retries/fallback/streaming counts
3) add clustering/partition scheme + backfill last 30d for beta accounts only
4) for latency: choose histogram buckets (fast) now; consider t-digest later

Allison Grant: add alerting around agg lag + query p95. if we’re doing MVs, we need freshness indicators in console anyway.

Chloe Martin: i’ll take (1) + coordinate with Felix on query service changes. Logan/Mei can you spec the MV/job columns today so we can update ENG-3812?

Mei Lin: yep, I’ll post a proposed schema in the ENG-3812 ticket comments.

Thomas Baird: i can run a quick benchmark once schema is set. we should test 7d/30d scans on a “big” workspace and confirm p95 < 1.5s for interactive.

Dylan Brooks: 👍 let’s do it. also: for beta, we can message “30d+ recommended via export.” will sync w/ Marissa/Paula.

Nadia Rahman: one more note: reconciliation vs billing—ensure aggregation uses the same rounding policy as billing hooks (or stores unrounded units and only rounds at display/export). don’t let perf changes break correctness.

Chloe Martin: good call. we’ll store raw microdollars/token_units in agg and format at the edge.

Felix Schneider: I’ll open a PR to remove timezone transforms from WHERE and to enforce a max time range for interactive endpoints (configurable) until MV is live.

Allison Grant: please also log `warehouse_bytes_scanned` and `warehouse_query_time_ms` per request in the query service. makes perf regressions obvious.

Dylan Brooks: done. closing loop once we have new benchmark numbers.

title: #eng-platform: 1737924411
preview:
Logan Wright: heads up: I think we have a gap for Console results dashboard after the attribution schema update. We added `{experiment_id, variant_id}` dims to the rollups, but existing rollup partitions (and some downstream cached datasets) won’t have those columns populated -> Console is going to show “no data” / flatlines for anything before deploy.

Mei Lin: yep, confirmed. The `route_variant_metrics_rollup` table is partitioned by hour and the new columns are NULL for pre-change partitions. The results API query groups by variant, so it drops the old partitions on the floor.

Chloe Martin: that explains what I’m seeing in staging — list/detail loads but charts only start at ~this morning. I assumed it was just low traffic :facepalm:

Caleb Johnson: +1. Ingestion is fine; it’s the aggregation layer. Raw spans have the attrs for ~N days, but the rollup job only started writing the new schema when we flipped it.

Dylan Brooks: do we need a backfill for beta/GA? If we only show data “since deploy” it’s going to be super confusing, esp for experiments that started earlier in the week.

Nadia Rahman: for cost/token deltas we also rely on the same rollups. If we don’t backfill, the cost panel will look like it’s missing historical baseline.

Allison Grant: from an observability standpoint, we should be explicit: what’s the retention for raw that we can backfill from? We can’t conjure attribution dims if they weren’t on the spans.

Caleb Johnson: good news: the dims *were* on the OTel spans starting ~Jan 20 (router context plumbing). The schema change here is basically “rollups now include the dims”. So we can recompute rollups from raw spans for the overlap window.

Mei Lin: constraints:
- raw trace/span retention in the analytics bucket is 14 days (rolling)
- long-term metrics store has 30 days, but it didn’t include the new tags until very recently (and we intentionally sampled some high-cardinality tags)
So practical backfill is “last 10-14 days” max.

Logan Wright: ok, so proposal:
1) run a one-off backfill job to rebuild rollup partitions for the last 14 days from the raw span export (where `experiment_id/variant_id` exist)
2) rerun downstream materialized views / cached datasets used by results API
3) add a banner in Console: “Experiment metrics available from <timestamp>” if older than backfill window

Rafael Mendes: +1 and we should add an alert for “results dashboard empty while traffic > 0” to catch this earlier next time.

Jada Williams: careful: reprocessing old partitions has to be idempotent + won’t double count. Recommend writing to `*_v2` temp table and swap, or do partition overwrite with explicit `run_id` guard.

Mei Lin: I can do partition overwrite in the warehouse safely as long as we lock to a bounded time range and disable the incremental job during overwrite.

Dylan Brooks: what’s the cost/ETA?

Logan Wright: rough math from yesterday’s span volumes:
- ~270M request-level span rows over 14d across prod (post-sampling), plus token spans are way bigger but rollup only needs request spans + cost records.
- scan size ~6.5–8.0 TB depending on predicate pushdown
- warehouse estimate: ~$650–$900 in compute + storage churn (assuming we do it in 6-hour chunks to avoid long queries)
- runtime: ~4–6 hours wall-clock if we parallelize by day

Caleb Johnson: that’s acceptable for beta. We should also throttle to keep from stepping on other ETLs (we already had query cost spikes when results queries got expensive).

Chloe Martin: for Console, if backfill completes today we can remove a bunch of “why is it blank” support churn. Support already pinged me about the charts.

Connor O'Brien: if we’re doing this, I need it on the release checklist. Also we should document that schema changes to attribution dims require backfill as a standard step.

Allison Grant: also: make sure the backfill doesn’t reintroduce high-cardinality tags into metrics streams we purposely restricted. Keep it to the canonical dims only: `{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region}`.

Mei Lin: action items proposal:
- Mei: draft the backfill SQL + runbook steps (partition overwrite + disable/enable incremental rollup job)
- Logan: sanity-check cost estimate + get quick :eyes: from SRE re: warehouse load window
- Caleb: confirm raw span export has stable field names + no missing attrs for that window
- Chloe: add temporary UI note “metrics available since…” + handle empty state copy
- Connor: add checklist item + coordinate timing with release train

Logan Wright: sounds good. I’ll file this as ENG-9034 if it isn’t already, and link in here.

Rafael Mendes: please include a rollback: if backfill starts causing warehouse pain, stop after N days and at least backfill the last 48h.

Caleb Johnson: one more gotcha: attribution drop rate was non-trivial on streaming retries earlier this week. Backfill won’t fix missing attribution; it’ll just recompute what exists. So results might still show some “unattributed” bucket unless we filter it.

Jada Williams: yeah, for results UI we should either (a) exclude unattributed from variant comparison and show a small “X% unattributed” indicator, or (b) treat it as a data quality guardrail.

Chloe Martin: I’ll add a small callout. We already have guardrail banners; can reuse pattern.

Logan Wright: ok. target: start backfill tonight 7pm PT off-peak. I’ll post when the job is queued.

—

Connor O'Brien: (next morning) did it run?

Mei Lin: kicked off at 19:12 PT, finished 00:48 PT. Overwrote 14 days of partitions. Total scan 7.4TB. Compute cost came out to ~$812.

Caleb Johnson: spot checked attribution completeness: last 7d looks good; before Jan 20 there’s still basically nothing (as expected).

Chloe Martin: Console charts now show data back to Jan 20 for my test experiment. Empty-state copy updated for earlier windows. :thumbsup:

Logan Wright: great. I’ll add a follow-up to formalize “schema change -> backfill plan” in the metrics taxonomy doc + release checklist.
text:
Logan Wright: heads up: I think we have a gap for Console results dashboard after the attribution schema update. We added `{experiment_id, variant_id}` dims to the rollups, but existing rollup partitions (and some downstream cached datasets) won’t have those columns populated -> Console is going to show “no data” / flatlines for anything before deploy.

Mei Lin: yep, confirmed. The `route_variant_metrics_rollup` table is partitioned by hour and the new columns are NULL for pre-change partitions. The results API query groups by variant, so it drops the old partitions on the floor.

Chloe Martin: that explains what I’m seeing in staging — list/detail loads but charts only start at ~this morning. I assumed it was just low traffic :facepalm:

Caleb Johnson: +1. Ingestion is fine; it’s the aggregation layer. Raw spans have the attrs for ~N days, but the rollup job only started writing the new schema when we flipped it.

Dylan Brooks: do we need a backfill for beta/GA? If we only show data “since deploy” it’s going to be super confusing, esp for experiments that started earlier in the week.

Nadia Rahman: for cost/token deltas we also rely on the same rollups. If we don’t backfill, the cost panel will look like it’s missing historical baseline.

Allison Grant: from an observability standpoint, we should be explicit: what’s the retention for raw that we can backfill from? We can’t conjure attribution dims if they weren’t on the spans.

Caleb Johnson: good news: the dims *were* on the OTel spans starting ~Jan 20 (router context plumbing). The schema change here is basically “rollups now include the dims”. So we can recompute rollups from raw spans for the overlap window.

Mei Lin: constraints:
- raw trace/span retention in the analytics bucket is 14 days (rolling)
- long-term metrics store has 30 days, but it didn’t include the new tags until very recently (and we intentionally sampled some high-cardinality tags)

title: #incidents: 1737851020
preview:
Incident Bot: :rotating_light: Triggered: **Unit Economics pipeline - token volume drop**
Alert: `unit_econ.output_tokens_hourly` down >35% vs baseline
Window: last 2 hours
Dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.dev/d/unit-econ-pipeline
Runbook: https://confluence.redwood.dev/display/SRE/Unit+Economics+Pipeline+Oncall

Sean Gallagher: grabbing. seeing a cliff starting ~06:00 UTC. anyone else online?

Allison Grant: here. i’m looking at metering ingestion lag first. the unit-econ agg job might be fine but starved.

Aisha Bello: confirming alert fired from the completeness check (hourly). also got the “missing hours” secondary alert.

Logan Wright: telemetry side checking now. quick triage: is it *only* output tokens or all token types?

Nadia Rahman: i pulled metering topic stats. `metering_events_v2` consumer lag spiked around 05:47 UTC. output token events appear delayed (not absent).

Sean Gallagher: ok so likely ingestion lag -> missing hours in warehouse views -> dashboard shows drop. any infra changes?

Allison Grant: looks like `metering-ingest-prod` restarted 2x. also redpanda partition 12 had leader move. could be causing catch-up.

Mei Lin: warehouse side: `unit_econ_hourly_v1` has gaps for 06:00 and 07:00 (UTC). job log says “0 rows scanned” for those partitions.

Caleb Johnson: data quality check is marking `coverage_ratio` at 0.62 for the hour because missing region/tier rows. it’s failing fast, not just partial.

Sean Gallagher: impact assessment: internal dashboard + any exec report today?

Rishi Malhotra: jumping in—FP&A was pulling QTD this morning. if the hours are missing it’ll look like cost/tokens blew up. not customer-facing but we need numbers backfilled + note for Finance.

Chloe Martin: Console page is showing a huge spike in $/1M output tokens because denominator dropped. users already pinging in #finance.

Sean Gallagher: ack. treat as SEV-2 (internal reporting impact). goal: restore completeness + backfill, then post note.

Incident Bot: Status update requested by @sean-gallagher

Logan Wright: root cause hypothesis: ingestion lag in `metering-ingest` consumer group. action: confirm consumers healthy + re-run aggregation for missing hours.

Nadia Rahman: consumer lag currently decreasing. from ~3.8M msgs to ~1.1M in last 10 min. ETA ~20-30 min if it keeps rate.

Allison Grant: i can scale consumers temporarily. bumping `metering-ingest-prod` from 12 -> 24 replicas.

Sean Gallagher: do it. also can we suppress the noisy alerts until lag clears?

Aisha Bello: i can snooze the unit-econ completeness alerts for 60 mins. will leave “freshness” alert on.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup:

Aisha Bello: snoozed alert `unit-econ-hourly-completeness` until 08:30 UTC.

Mei Lin: once ingestion catches up, we still need to backfill the hourly aggregates. the regular job won’t automatically recompute past partitions (it’s idempotent but only runs current hour).

Caleb Johnson: +1. also, if we backfill, we should re-run the reconciliation view to ensure no weird deltas.

Cole Summers: cost attribution inputs (rate card + overhead alloc) are present for the day. so backfill should be straightforward; nothing missing on spend side.

Sean Gallagher: plan:
1) wait for metering ingestion lag to get near-zero
2) run backfill for affected range (looks like 06:00-07:59 UTC?)
3) verify coverage + dashboard normalization
4) update Finance

Logan Wright: i’ll confirm exact missing hours via query. running:
`select hour, sum(output_tokens) from analytics.unit_econ_hourly_v1 where hour >= '2025-01-25 05:00' and hour < '2025-01-25 09:00' group by 1 order by 1;`

Nadia Rahman: lag now ~210k msgs. scaling helped.

Allison Grant: pods up. consumer throughput doubled. no error spikes.

Logan Wright: query results: 05:00 ok, 06:00 = 0, 07:00 = 0, 08:00 partial (still ingesting). so backfill 06:00-08:00 once ingestion finishes.

Sean Gallagher: great. can we define “ingestion finished” as consumer lag <10k + metering_events count looks normal for current hour?

Nadia Rahman: yes. also checking parity metric `output_token_events / request_count` is back to baseline.

Chloe Martin: FYI dashboard has caching/batching charts blank for those hours too (same underlying tables). makes sense.

Mei Lin: i can run backfill once green. command from ENG-8433 tooling:
`./bin/unit_econ_backfill --env=prod --start='2025-01-25T06:00:00Z' --end='2025-01-25T09:00:00Z' --granularity=hour --throttle=low`
Will do dry-run first.

Sean Gallagher: please do, and run in low throttle. last time backfill hammered the warehouse.

Caleb Johnson: when it’s done, i’ll check:
- completeness: `coverage_ratio` >= 0.98
- missing dims < thresholds
- “sudden drop” detector clears

Incident Bot: :information_source: SEV-2 declared by @sean-gallagher. Primary: restore unit economics hourly completeness. Next update in 30m.

Nadia Rahman: consumer lag is now 4.3k. basically caught up.

Allison Grant: confirm stable. reverting replicas back to 12 in ~15 min if no re-lag.

Mei Lin: running dry-run backfill now.
`./bin/unit_econ_backfill ... --dry-run`
Dry-run output: will recompute 3 partitions (06,07,08). estimated runtime 11-14 min. proceeding non-dry-run.

Sean Gallagher: ok.

Mei Lin: backfill started (job id `ue_backfill_20250125_0600`).

Logan Wright: while backfill runs, i’m writing a short note for #finance: metric artifact due to ingestion lag; hours will be repopulated shortly.

Rishi Malhotra: thanks. please include which hours + that it’s *denominator drop* so $/1M spike is not real.

Caleb Johnson: seeing the pipeline checks still red for 06/07 (expected). will refresh after job completes.

Aisha Bello: FYI snooze still active; i’ll unsnooze as soon as completeness passes so we catch any follow-on.

Mei Lin: backfill completed. rows written:
- 06:00: 1,284 rows
- 07:00: 1,301 rows
- 08:00: 1,279 rows
No errors.

Caleb Johnson: rerunning validation query + checks.

Caleb Johnson: checks now green. `coverage_ratio` 0.991 / 0.993 / 0.989 for 06/07/08. “missing hours” detector cleared. :thumbsup:

Chloe Martin: dashboard refreshed (hard reload) and the $/1M chart looks normal again. spike gone.

Allison Grant: scaling reverted to 12 replicas. lag holding at ~0.

Aisha Bello: unsnoozed `unit-econ-hourly-completeness`.

Sean Gallagher: nice. closeout: root cause ingestion lag due to partition leader move + consumer restarts; mitigation scale consumers; fix/backfill. Logan can you post finance note?

Logan Wright: posted in #finance: hours 06:00-08:00 UTC on 2025-01-25 were delayed; backfill completed; dashboard normalized; no actual cost change.

Rishi Malhotra: confirmed. QTD looks sane again. thanks all.

Incident Bot: :white_check_mark: Resolved by @sean-gallagher. Duration: 3h 3m.

Sean Gallagher: follow-ups:
- Nadia/Allison: why did `metering-ingest-prod` restart twice? add guardrails / alert earlier on lag.
- Mei: document backfill steps + safe throttle defaults in runbook.
- Caleb: add note in anomaly alert payload when denominator drop likely due to missing hours (so folks don’t panic on $/1M spike).
Linking to ENG ticket(s) in separate thread once created.
